question: In the presence of a catalyst, activation energy of a reaction is lowered by 2 k cal at $27 ^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Hence, rate will be
explanation:
Arrhenius equation is
${\rm{k}} = {\rm{A}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{E}}_{\rm{a}}}{\rm{/RT}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (1)$
In the presence of catalyst,rate constant
${{\rm{k}}^\prime } = {\rm{A}}{{\rm{e}}^{ - {\rm{E}}_a^\prime {\rm{/ RT}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2)$
On dividing Eq. (2) by Eq. (1), we get
$\frac{{{{\text{k}}^\prime }}}{{\text{k}}} = {{\text{e}}^{\left( {{{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}} - {{\text{E}}_a}^\prime } \right)}}/{\text{RT}}$
$\frac{{{{\text{k}}^\prime }}}{{\text{k}}} = {{\text{e}}^{\left( {\frac{{2000}}{{2 \times 300}}} \right)}} = {{\text{e}}^{3.33}}$
$\left[ {\because {{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}} - {{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}^\prime = 2000\,{\text{cal (given)}}} \right]$
$\because$ On taking log both sides,
$\log \frac{{{{\text{k}}^\prime }}}{{\text{k}}} = \frac{{3.33}}{{2.303}}$
$\frac{{{{\text{k}}^\prime }}}{{\text{k}}} = \operatorname{antilog} 1.446 = 27.92 \approx 28$
We know that, rate ${\text{(R)}} \propto {\text{k}}$
$\therefore \quad {{\text{R}}^\prime }/{\text{R}} = {{\text{k}}^\prime }/{\text{k}} = 28$
$\therefore \quad {{\text{R}}^\prime } = 28{\text{R}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Consider the following reaction that goes from A to B in three steps as shown below:

https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764948872/preptracker/questions/q_24311_explanation_1764948871737.png

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: If the activation energy of a reaction is zero, temperature will have no effect on the rate constant.


Reason: Lower the activation energy, faster is the reaction.
explanation:
According to the Arrhenius equation, the rate constant $(\mathrm{k})$ of a chemical reaction is related to the activation energy $(\mathrm{Ea})$, the gas constant $(\mathrm{R})$, the absolute temperature $(\mathrm{T})$, and the pre-exponential factor (A). The equation is expressed as follows:
$\mathrm{K}=\mathrm{Ae}^{-\mathrm{Ea} / \mathrm{RT}}$
At an absolute temperature of 0 Kelvin $(\mathrm{E}=0)$, the rate constant is equal to the pre-exponential factor, A. This represents the lowest possible temperature at which a chemical reaction can occur. As the Ea decreases, no of molecules with Ea increases, as a result rate increases. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question: At $300 \mathrm{~K}$, rate constant for $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ product at $\mathrm{t}=50 \mathrm{~min}$ is $0.02 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, then rate constant at $\mathrm{t}=$ $75 \mathrm{~min}$ and $310 \mathrm{~K}$ will be (in $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ )

At $\mathrm{t}=50 \mathrm{sec}, 300 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{k}=0.02 \mathrm{sec}^{-}$
$\mathrm{t}=75 \mathrm{sec}, 310 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{k}=$ ?
Rate constant does not change with time but changes with temperature for every $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ rise in temperature, rate constant doubles (in general). So, $2 \times 0.02=0.04 \mathrm{sec}^{-}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The activation energy of a reaction at a given temperature is found to be ${\text{2}}{\text{.303 RT J mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$. The ratio of rate constant $(\mathrm{k})$ to the arrhenius factor (A) is:
explanation:
$\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{Ae}^{-\mathrm{Ea} / \mathrm{RT}}$
$\log \mathrm{k}=\log \mathrm{A}-\dfrac{\mathrm{Ea}}{\mathrm{RT} \times 2.303}$
$\log \mathrm{k}-\log \mathrm{A}=\dfrac{-\mathrm{Ea}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}$
$\log \dfrac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{A}}=\dfrac{-\mathrm{Ea}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}=\dfrac{-2.303 \mathrm{RT}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{A}}=\operatorname{anti} \log -1=1 \times 10^{-1}=0.1$
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate constant for a first order reaction at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for which $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}$ is $35 \mathrm{Kcal}^{\mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$ and frequency constant ' $A$ ' is $1.45 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$ is:
explanation:
$\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{Ae}^{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}} / \mathrm{RT}}$
$\log \mathrm{k}=\log \left(1.45 \times 10^{11}\right)-\dfrac{35 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 2.303 \times 573}$
$\left[ {\because {\text{R}} = 2\,\,{\text{cal}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}{{\text{k}}^{ - 1}}} \right]$
$\log \mathrm{k}=11.16-13.26=-2.1$
Taking antilog, $\mathrm{k}=7.94 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the activation energy $(\mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol})$ for a reaction, if its rate constant doubles when the temperature is raised from $300 \mathrm{~K}$ to $400 \mathrm{~K}$ $\left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{Jmol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
$\log \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right]$ where $\mathrm{T}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{T}_{2}$ are initial and final temperature; $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ are initial and final rate constants; $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=$ activation energy and $\mathrm{R}=$ gas constant.
Given, $\mathrm{T}_{1}=300 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~T}_{2}=400 \mathrm{~K}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \\& \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}=\dfrac{2}{1}\end{aligned}$
$\log \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2303 \times \mathrm{R}}\left[\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right]$
Thus, $\log \dfrac{2}{1}=\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\dfrac{1}{300}-\dfrac{1}{400}\right]$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=0.30 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 3 \times 400$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=6.88 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question:

Reason: Both the reactions have same activation energy
explanation: Both the reactions $2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ and $2 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ proceed at the different rates and have different activation energies.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are  incorrect.

question:
The rate constant for the first order decomposition of a certain reaction is described by the equation
$\ln \mathrm{k}\left(\mathrm{s}^{-1}\right)=14.34-\dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~K}}{\mathrm{~T}}$. The energy of activation for this reaction is
explanation:
Given that,
In ${\text{k}}\left( {{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}} \right) = 14.34 - \frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}}}{{\,{\text{T}}}} {\text{K}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(1)}}$
We know that,
In ${\text{k}}\left( {{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}} \right) = \ln {\text{A}} - \frac{{{{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}}}{{{\text{RT}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(2)}}$
On comparing equation (1) and (2) we get
$\frac{{{{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}}}{{\text{R}}} = 1.25 \times {10^4} {\text{K}}$
$\therefore {{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}} = 1.25 \times {10^4} {\text{K}} \times {\text{R cal }}{{\text{K}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}{\text{ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$
$ = 1.25 \times {10^4}\,{\text{K}} \times {\text{2 cal }}{{\text{K}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}{\text{ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$ $\left[\because \mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
$ = 2.50 \times {10^4}{\text{cal mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Rate constant $(\mathrm{k})$ varies with temperature as given by equation $\log \mathrm{k}\left(\mathrm{min}^{-1}\right)=5-\dfrac{2000}{\mathrm{~T}}$. Consider the following about this equation
I. Pre-exponential factor is $10^{5}$.
II. $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}$ is $9.212 \mathrm{kcal}$.
III. Variation of $\log \mathrm{k}$ with $\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$ is linear.
Select the correct statements.
explanation:
Given,
$\log {\text{k}}\left( {{{\min }^{ - 1}}} \right) = 5 - \frac{{2000}}{{\,{\text{T}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(1)}}$
$\because {\log _{10}}{\text{k}} = {\log _{10}}{\text{A}} - \frac{{{{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}}}{{2.303{\text{RT}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(2)}}$
On comparing equations (1) and (2)
I. $\log \mathrm{A}=5$ or $\mathrm{A}=10^{5}$
II. $\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}=2000$
$ \Rightarrow {{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}{\text{ = 2}}{\text{.303}} \times {\text{2000}} \times {\text{0}}{\text{.002 kcal}}$
$\therefore {{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}} = {\text{9}}{\text{.212 k cal}}$
III. From Eq. (2), it is clear that,
$\log \mathrm{k} \propto \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$
$\therefore$ All given statements are correct.
subject: Chemistry

question: The temperature dependence of rate constant $(\mathrm{k})$ of a chemical reaction is written in terms of Arrhenius equation $\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{Ae}^{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}} / \mathrm{RT}}$. Activation energy $\left(E_{a}\right)$ of the reaction can be calculated by plotting.
explanation:
$\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{Ae}^{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}} / \mathrm{RT}}$
$\therefore \log \mathrm{k}=\log \mathrm{A}-\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}$
Comparing this equation with straight line
$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{c}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\log \mathrm{k}$ and $\mathrm{x}=1 / \mathrm{T}$
$\therefore \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}$ is calculated with the help of slope by plotting the curve between $\log \mathrm{k}$ and $\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate constants $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ for two different reactions are $10^{16} \times \mathrm{e}^{-2000 / \mathrm{T}}$ and $10^{15} \times \mathrm{e}^{-1000 / \mathrm{T}}$ respectively. The temperature at which $\mathrm{k}_{1}=\mathrm{k}_{2}$ is:
explanation:
$\mathrm{k}_{1}=10^{16} \times \mathrm{e}^{\frac{-2000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
$\mathrm{k}_{2}=10^{15} \times \mathrm{e}^{\frac{-1000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
Given, $\mathrm{k}_{1}=\mathrm{k}_{2}$
$10^{16} \times \mathrm{e}^{\frac{-2000}{\mathrm{~T}}}=10^{15} \times \mathrm{e}^{\frac{-1000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
${\text{10 = }}{{\text{e}}^{\frac{{{\text{1000}}}}{{{\text{T}}}}}}$
$\ln 10=\dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~T}}$
$2.303 \log 10=\dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~T}}$
$\mathrm{T}=\dfrac{1000}{2.303} \mathrm{k}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For a reaction, the value of rate constant at 300 $\mathrm{K}$ is $6.0 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The value of Arrhenius factor $\mathrm{A}$, at infinitely high temperature is:

At $\mathrm{T}=\infty ; \mathrm{k}=\mathrm{A}$

question:
For the equilibrium,
${\text{A}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{(g)}} \rightleftharpoons {\text{B}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{(g),}}\,$ ${\rm{\Delta H = - 40}}\,{\rm{kJ/mol}}$. If the ratio of the activation energies of the forward $\left( {{{\text{E}}_{\text{f}}}} \right)$ and reverse $\left( {{{\text{E}}_{\text{b}}}} \right)$ reactions is $\frac{{\text{2}}}{{\text{3}}}$ then
explanation:
Given, $\Delta H=-40 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\dfrac{E_{f}}{E_{b}}=\dfrac{2}{3} \text { or } E_{f}=\dfrac{2}{3} E_{b}$
we know that,
$\Delta H=E_{f}-E_{b}=-40$
$\dfrac{2}{3} E_{b}-E_{b}=-40$
$E_{b}=120 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$E_{f}=\dfrac{2}{3} \times 120$
$=80 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The rate constant for the first order decomposition of the ethylene oxide into $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{CO}$ is described by $\log \mathrm{k}\left(\mathrm{s}^{-1}\right)=14.34-\dfrac{12.5 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~K}}{\mathrm{~T}}$
Then the activation energy of the reaction is
explanation:
$\log \mathrm{k}=\log \mathrm{A}-\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}$
$\log \mathrm{k}=14.34-\dfrac{12.5 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~K}}{\mathrm{~T}}$
From Eqs. (1) and (2)
$\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}=12.5 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=\left(12.5 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~K}\right) \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$=239 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$=2.39 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$=2.39 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

(ii) For 2-butene nitrile ; ${\text{k'}}\left( {{{\text{s}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}} \right){\text{ = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{11}}}}{\text{ex}}{{\text{p}}^{{\text{ - 214}}{\text{.5kmo}}{{\text{\Gamma }}^{{\text{ - 1/RT}}}}}}$
The temperature at which ${\text{k = k'}}$ is
explanation:
$10^{13.8} \exp ^{-263.5 k J m o \Gamma^{1 / R T}}=10^{11} \exp ^{-214.5 k J m o I^{-1 / R T}}$
or
$\begin{gathered}10^{13.8-11}=\exp \dfrac{[(-214.5)-(-263.5)] \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}}{8.314 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \times T} \\\quad \text { or } 630.957=\exp \dfrac{5.893 \times 10^{3}}{T} \\\therefore 2.303 \log 630.957=\dfrac{5.893 \times 10^{3}}{T} \\\therefore \quad T=\dfrac{5.893 \times 10^{3}}{2.303 \times 2.8}=913.87 \mathrm{~K}\end{gathered}$

question: Activation energy $\left( {{{\rm{E}}_{\rm{a}}}} \right)$ and rate constants ${\rm{(}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{)}}$ and $\left.k_{2}\right)$ of a chemical reaction at two different temperatures $\left( {{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right)$ and $\left( {{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right)$are related by
explanation:
On integrating within limits $k_{1}$ to $k_{2}$ and $T_{1}$ to $T_{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \int_{k_{1}}^{k_{2}} \ln k=-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R} \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \dfrac{1}{T} \\& \ln \dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\left[\dfrac{1}{T_{2}}-\dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right]=\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\left[\dfrac{1}{T_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{T_{2}}\right] \\& \text { or } \ln \dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\left[\dfrac{1}{T_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{T_{2}}\right]\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Two reactions ${{\rm{R}}_{\rm{1}}}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{{\rm{R}}_{\rm{2}}}$ have identical preexponential factors. Activation energy of ${{\rm{R}}_{\rm{1}}}$ exceeds that of ${{\text{R}}_{\text{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{by10}}\,{\text{kJ}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$.
If ${{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}$ and ${{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}}$ and ${{\text{R}}_{\text{2}}}$ are rate constants for reaction ${{\rm{R}}_{\rm{1}}}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{{\rm{R}}_{\rm{2}}}$ respectively at 300 k, then ${\rm{in}}\left( {{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{/}}{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right)$ is equal to :- $\left( {{\rm{R = 8}}{\rm{.314\,J\,mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{\,}}{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}} \right)$
explanation:
From Arrhenius equation,
$\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\dfrac{-\mathrm{Ea}}{\mathrm{RT}}}$
so,
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{k}_{1}=\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{1 /} / \mathrm{RT}}} \\& \mathrm{k}_{2}=\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{2} / \mathrm{RT}}}\end{aligned}$
so, equation (2)/ (1) $\Rightarrow \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}=\mathrm{e}^{\dfrac{\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}-\mathrm{Ea}_{2}\right)}{\mathrm{RT}}}$
(As pre-exponential factors of both reactions is same)
$\ln \left(\dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}\right)=\dfrac{\mathrm{Ea}_{1}-\mathrm{Ea}_{2}}{\mathrm{RT}}=\dfrac{10000}{8.314 \times 300}=4$
subject: Chemistry

question:
A reactant (1) forms two products
${\text{A}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}}}{\text{B}}$, Activation energy ${{\text{E}}_{{{\text{a}}_{\text{1}}}}}$
${\text{A}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{C}}$, Activation energy ${{\text{E}}_{{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}}}$
If ${\left( {{{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = 2}}{\left( {{{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}} \right)_{\text{1}}}$ then ${{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}}$ are related as
explanation:
$A \stackrel{k_{1}}{\longrightarrow} B, A \stackrel{k_{2}}{\longrightarrow} C$
By Arrhenius equation,
$\begin{aligned}& k_{1}=A^{\prime} e^{-E_{\alpha_{l}} / R T} \\& \therefore \quad E_{a_{2}}=2 E_{a_{1}} \\& \therefore \quad k_{2}=A^{\prime} e^{-E_{a_{1}} / R T} \\& \dfrac{k_{1}}{k_{2}}=\dfrac{A^{\prime} e^{-E_{q_{1}} / R T}}{A^{\prime} e^{-E_{\alpha_{2}} / R T}}=e^{E_{q_{1}} / R T} \\& \therefore \quad k_{1}=k_{2} e^{E_{q /} / R T}\end{aligned}$
and $k_{2}=A^{\prime} e^{-E_{q_{2}} / R T}\left[A^{\prime}\right.$ is Arrhenius constant $]$
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate constant $(\mathrm{k})$ for a particular reaction is $1.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at $\quad 100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \quad$ and $1.3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at $150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the energy of activation $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}\right)$ (in $\mathrm{kJ}$ ) for the reaction? $\left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
$\log \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right]$
$\begin{aligned}& \log \dfrac{1.3 \times 10^{-3}}{1.3 \times 10^{-4}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\dfrac{1}{373}-\dfrac{1}{423}\right] \\& 1=\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\dfrac{50}{373 \times 423}\right] \\& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 373 \times 423}{1000 \times 50} \\& =60.42 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$

question: The temperature coefficient of a reaction is 2 . When the temperature is increased from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the rate of reaction is increased by
explanation:
Temperature coefficient 2 means rate of reaction doubles at every $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ rise in temperature.

question: The activation energy of a reaction is zero. Its rate constant at $280 \mathrm{~K}$ is $1.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, the rate constant at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is
explanation:
Given,
Activation energy of a reaction, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}=0$
Rate constant, $\mathrm{k}_{1}=1.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
Temperature, $\mathrm{T}_{1}=280 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~T}_{2}=300 \mathrm{~K}$
According to Arrhenius equation
$\begin{gathered}\log \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{1.6 \times 10^{-6}}=\dfrac{0}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\dfrac{1}{280}-\dfrac{1}{300}\right] \\\log \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{1.6 \times 10^{-6}} 0 \\\dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{1.6 \times 10^{-6}}=\text { antilog } 0 \\\dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{1.6 \times 10^{-6}}=1 \\\therefore \quad \mathrm{k}_{2}=1.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\end{gathered}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The activation energy for a reaction at the temperature TK was found to be ${\rm{2}}{\rm{.303}}\,\,{\rm{RTJ}}\,\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$. The ratio of the rate constant to Arrhenius factor is
explanation:
${\text{2}}{\text{.303}}\,{\text{log}}\,{\text{k = 2}}{\text{.303}}\,{\text{log}}\,{\text{A - }}\frac{{{{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}}}{{{\text{RT}}}}$
${{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}{\text{ = 2}}{\text{.303}}\,{\text{RT}}\,\,{\text{(given)}}$
Putting the values of ${{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}$ and dividing by 2.303 on both sides, we get,
log k = log A - 1;log k - log A = -1
${\text{log}}\left( {\frac{{\text{k}}}{{\text{A}}}} \right){\text{ = - 1}}$
Taking antilog $\left( {\frac{{\text{k}}}{{\text{A}}}} \right){\text{ = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which among the following equation represents Arrhenius equation?
explanation:
Variation of rate constant $\mathrm{k}$ with temperature $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ is given by Arrhenius equation.
It is based on the two theories of reaction rates, i.e., collision theory and transition state theory.
${\rm{k = A}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{E}}_{\rm{a}}}{\rm{/RT}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{A}}}{{{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{Ea/RT}}}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(1)}}$
where ' $A$ ' is called the frequency factor or the pre-exponential factor.
$E_{a}$ is activation energy $e^{-E_{a} / R T}$ is referred as Boltzmann factor. A, has same unit as the rate constant. Collectively $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}$ are called the Arrhenius parameters of the reaction.
subject: Chemistry

question: Temperature coefficient of a reaction is ' 2 '. When temperature is increased from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $90^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the rate of reaction is increased by

question: Slope of the straight line obtained by plotting $\log _{10} \mathrm{k}$ against $1 / \mathrm{T}$ represents what term?
explanation:
According to Arrhenius equation,
$\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{Ae}^{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}} / \mathrm{RT}}$
By taking log on both sides
$\log \mathrm{k}=\log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{A}-\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}$
$\log \mathrm{k}=\log \mathrm{A}-\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}} \cdot \dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2 \cdot 303 \mathrm{R}}$
Comparing it with $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{c}$
A plot between $\log _{10} k$ and $\left(\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}\right)$ is a straight line and slope is given by https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953996/preptracker/questions/q_22549_explanation_1764953995720.png $\text { Slope }=\dfrac{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}$
(Slope has negative value)

question: What is the activation energy for a reaction if its rate doubles when the temperature is raised from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $35^{\circ} \mathrm{C} ?\left(R=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
$\log \dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\dfrac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{T_{2}}\right)$
$\begin{gathered}\log 2=\dfrac{E_{a}}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left[\dfrac{1}{293}-\dfrac{1}{308}\right] \\0.3=\dfrac{E_{a}}{2.303 \times 8.314} \times \dfrac{15}{293 \times 308} \\E_{a}=\dfrac{0.3 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 308}{15} \\=34673 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}=34.7 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{gathered}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A given sample of milk turns sour at room temperature $\left(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ in 5 hours. In a refrigerator at $-3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, it can be stored 10 times longer. The energy of activation for the souring of milk is
explanation:
Rate of souring of milk at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is 10 times faster than at $-3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
hence, $\dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{300}}{\mathrm{k}_{270}}=10, \mathrm{~T}_{1}=270 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~T}_{2}=300 \mathrm{~K}$ $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=2.303 \mathrm{R}\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{T}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{2}}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}-\mathrm{T}_{1}}\right) \log \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{300}}{\mathrm{k}_{270}}$
$E_{a}=2.303 R\left(\dfrac{270 \times 300}{300-270}\right) \log 10$
$E_{a}=2.303 \mathrm{R}\left(2.7 \times 10^{3}\right) \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=2.303 \times 2.7 \mathrm{R} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$

$\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\text { Catalyst } 2} \mathrm{P}_{2}, \mathrm{k}_{2}=0.1 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
Reaction 3:
$\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\text { Catalyst } 3} \mathrm{P}_{3}, \mathrm{k}_{3}=0.01 \mathrm{~L}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$

explanation:

$\mathrm{r}_{1}=10 \times \mathrm{r}_{2}$
$\mathrm{r}_{3}=\dfrac{\mathrm{r}_{2}}{10}$.

question: If the rate constant for a first order reaction is k,   the time $(\mathrm{t})$ required for the completion of  $99\,\% $  of the reaction is given by
explanation:
$\mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}} \log \dfrac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{R}]}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}} \log \left(\dfrac{100}{1}\right) \\& =\dfrac{4.606}{\mathrm{k}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The time required for $60\,\% $ completion of a first order reaction is 50 min . The time required for $93.6\% $ completion of the same reaction will be
explanation:
For first order reaction,
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}$
where, $\mathrm{a}=$ initial concentration of the reactant, $\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}=$ concentration of the reactant after time, t.
For ${\text{60% }}$ completion of reaction,$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{50} \log \dfrac{100}{40}$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{50} \times 0.397=0.0183$
For $93.6 \%$ completion of reaction,
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{100}{6.4}$
$0.0183=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{100}{6.4}$
$\mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{0.0183} \times 1.193$
$\mathrm{t}=150 \mathrm{~min}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In a first order reaction, the concentration of the reactant is reduced to $12.5\% $ in one hour. When was it half completed?
explanation:
For a first order reaction,
$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{[\mathrm{~A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]} & =\dfrac{2.303}{60} \log \dfrac{100}{12.5} \\& =0.0346 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
Now $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}=\dfrac{0.693}{0.0346}=20 \mathrm{~min}$

question: For a first order reaction, the graph between  $\log \,\,\frac{{\rm{a}}}{{({\rm{a}} - {\rm{x}})}}$  (on  ${\rm{y}}$ -axis) and time (in min, on ${\rm{x}}$  - axis) gave a straight line passing through origin. The slope is $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$. What is the rate constant (in $\mathrm{min}^{-1}$)?
explanation:
From first order reaction,
$\log \,\,\frac{{\rm{a}}}{{{\rm{a}} - {\rm{x}}}} = \frac{{{\rm{kt}}}}{{2.303}}$
${\rm{y}} = {\rm{mx}} + {\rm{c}}$
slope $(\mathrm{m})=\dfrac{\mathrm{k}}{2.303}$
Given, $\mathrm{m}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{k}}{2.303}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
$\therefore \mathrm{k}=4.606 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$

question: Match the following concepts from the topic Integrated Rate Equations and choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
The differential rate equation (A) is concerned with the instantaneous rate of reaction, which is determined by the slope of the tangent at a specific time in a concentration vs. time plot, corresponding to S.
The integrated rate equation (B) relates concentrations measured at different times to the rate constant, representing a relationship over time, hence R. Some examples of zero order reactions (C) are enzyme-catalyzed reactions and reactions on metal surfaces, making Q the correct match. The reaction order (D) cannot be theoretically predicted from the balanced equation alone; it must be determined by experiment, which is P. Correct option is (1)

options.text: A - S,        B - R, 	       C - Q,      D - P
A - Q, 	B - P,         C - S, 	 D - R
A - R, 	B - S, 	        C - P, 	 D - Q
A - S,  	B - R, 	        C - P, 	 D - Q

question: Match the following based on the topic Integrated Rate Equations and choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
The half-life $\left( {{{\text{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)$ concept (A) is common to both zero and first-order reactions and refers to the time it takes for the concentration of a reactant to reduce to half its original value $(\mathrm{R})$. The rate constant (k) for a first-order reaction (B) can be obtained from the graph of $\ln [\mathrm{R}]$ versus time, where it is the negative of the slope (S).
The units of the rate constant for a zero-order reaction (C) are given as the rate of change of concentration over time $(\mathrm{P})$, which is $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.
For a first-order reaction (D), the rate constant units are $\mathrm{s}^{-1}(\mathrm{Q})$, reflecting the exponential decay of reactant concentration over time. Correct option is (2)

options.text: A - P,        B - Q, 	     C - R,       D - S
A - R,       B - S, 	      C - P, 	D - Q
A - Q, 	B - P, 	      C - S, 	D - R
A - S,  	B - R, 	      C - Q, 	D - P

question: Match the following based on the topic Integrated Rate Equations and choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation: Zero order reactions have a rate that is independent of the concentration of reactants, hence  ${\text{S}}$  is the integrated rate equation for a zero   order reaction.  First - order reactions have a rate that is directly proportional to the concentration of one reactant, and R represents the integrated rate equation for a first-order reaction. For first-order reactions, the half-life is a constant Q , and it is given by the formula $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}$ $=0.693 / \mathrm{k}$.The rate constant k for a first-order reaction can be determined from the  $( - {\text{ve}})$  slope of the plot of $\ln [\mathrm{R}]$ versus time P.   Correct option is (4)

question: Match the following columns related to rate laws and reaction order and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
A matches with Q as a first order reaction has a rate that is directly proportional to the concentration of a single reactant.
B matches with P since the half-life is the time required for the concentration of a reactant to decrease to half of its initial value.
C matches with S because the rate constant (k) is the proportionality constant found in the rate law of a reaction.
D matches with R as the rate law represents the dependence of the reaction rate on the concentration of reactants. Correct option is (1)

" The reason given is that the integrated rate equation relates the concentration of reactants to time and the rate constant. This is also true and indeed the basis  for why the assertion is true. question: If benzene diazonium chloride undergoes first order decomposition at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ with a rate constant of $6.93 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$, the time for completion of $90 \%$ of the reaction (in min) is ____ (nearest integer)  $(\log 2=0.30, \log 3=0.477)$
explanation:
For $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction, ${\rm{t}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{\rm{k}}}\log \frac{{\left[ {{{\rm{A}}_0}} \right]}}{{[{\rm{A}}]}}$
$\mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{6.93 \times 10^{-2}} \log \left[\dfrac{100}{10}\right]=\dfrac{2.303}{6.93 \times 10^{-2}}$
$=33.23 \mathrm{~min} \approx 33 \mathrm{~min}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For a zero order reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ product, a plot of [A] (on y-axis) and time (on x-axis) gave a straight line with slope equal to $-3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{min}^{-1}$ and intercept equal to $2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$ (on Y-axis).   What is the rate constant (in $\mathrm{Mmin}^{-1}$) of this reaction?
explanation:
For zero order reaction,
$[\mathrm{A}]=-\mathrm{kt}+[\mathrm{A}]_{0}$
If we plot [A] against ${\text{t}},$ we get a straight line with slope equal to $ - {\text{k}}$ and intercept equal to $[\mathrm{A}]_{0}$ as shown in below
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952064/preptracker/questions/q_106821_explanation_1764952064461.png
$\therefore {\text{k}} = 3 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{\text{M}}\,\,{\text{mi}}{{\text{n}}^{ - 1}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate law for the decomposition of hydrogen iodide is $-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{HI}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}$. The units of rate constant k are
explanation:
Rate law $=-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{HI}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}$
It is a second order reaction i.e. $\mathrm{n}=2$.
$\therefore$ Units of $\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{L}^{\mathrm{n}-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{1-\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$
$\therefore \mathrm{k}=\mathrm{L} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{C}$ has a rate constant of $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. Which of the following is correct?
explanation:
From the units of k, it is $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction. For a first order reaction, the plot of $\ln [\mathrm{A}]$ vs t is a straight line with slope $=-\mathrm{k}$

question: Which of the following is a zero order reaction?

The photochemical reaction,
${{\text{H}}_2} + {\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}\xrightarrow{{{\text{hv}}}}2{\text{HCl}}$ is a zero order reaction as rate of reaction doesn't depend on concentration of reactants $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right.$ and $\left.\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)$. It depends on the incident light.
subject: Chemistry

question: For a first order reaction,  ${{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}$  is 1200 s . The specific rate constant in $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ is
explanation:
Given, ${{\text{t}}_{1/2}} = 1200\,{\text{s}}$
For $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction
$\mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}} \quad(\mathrm{k}=$ rate constant $)$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{0.693}{1200}=0.0005775=5.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: For a zero order reaction, the plot of concentration of reactant vs time is (Hint: Consider the intercept on the concentration axis)
explanation:
Any reaction of zero order must obey the following equation
$[\mathrm{A}]=-\mathrm{kt}+[\mathrm{A}]_{0}$ or $[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{A}]_{0}+\mathrm{kt}$
This shows that, the concentration of reactant decreases linearly with time, as it is an equation of a straight line ( $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{c}$ ), the plot of [A] versus t will be straight line with slope $=-\mathrm{k}$ and intercept on the concentration axis $=[\mathrm{A}]_{0}$ as shown in figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952057/preptracker/questions/q_106803_explanation_1764952056727.png

options.text: linear with  $ + {\rm{ve}}$  slope and non zero +ve intercept
linear with  $ - {\rm{ve}}$  slope and non zero  $ + {\rm{ve}}$  intercept
linear with  $ - {\rm{ve}}$  slope and zero intercept
linear with  $ + {\rm{ve}}$  slope and zero intercept

subject: Chemistry

question: For a zero-order reaction, the correct expression for rate constant (k) at half-life time  $({{\text{t}}_{1/2}})$  is   $(\,[{{\text{R}}_0}] = $  initial concentration of reactant)
explanation:
For zero-order reaction, integrated rate equation is given as:
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{R}_{0}\right]-[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{t}}$
When, ${\rm{t}} = {{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}$
$[\mathrm{R}]=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{R}_{0}\right]}{2}$
Thus, ${\rm{k}} = \frac{{\left[ {{{\rm{R}}_0}} \right] - \frac{1}{2}\left[ {{{\rm{R}}_0}} \right]}}{{{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}}}$
Hence, option (3) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry

question:
The decomposition of $\mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ follows first order kinetics and is given by $\mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
The rate constant for this reaction is$1.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The initial pressure of $\mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ is 100 atm .

question: The first order decomposition of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ in an appropriate medium is characterised by a rate constant of $0.2303 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$.  What is the time (in min) required to complete $9 / 10$  fraction of the reaction?
explanation:
$\mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})}$
$\mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{0.2303} \log \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{10}}=10 \mathrm{~min}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: A particular reaction has a rate constant $1.15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. How long does it take for 6 g of the reactant of reduce to 3 g ? $(\log 2=0.301)$
explanation: 6 g to 3 g means, half reaction is done. For a first order reaction,  ${{\text{t}}_{\frac{1}{2}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{\text{k}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{1.15 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\,{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}}} = 603\,\,{\text{sec}}.$

question: If the half-life $({{\rm{t}}_{1/2}})$  for a first order reaction is 1 minute, then the time required for $99.9 \%$ completion of the reaction is closest to :

subject: Chemistry

question:
If the rate constant of a reaction is $0.03 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, how much time does it take for $7.2 \mathrm{molL}^{-1}$ concentration of the reactant to get reduced to $0.9 \mathrm{molL}^{-1}$ ?
(Given: $\log 2=0.301$ )
explanation:
$\mathrm{k}=0.03 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{0.03} \log \dfrac{7.2}{0.9}$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{0.03} \log 8$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{0.03} \times 3 \times \log 2$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{0.03} \times 3 \times 0.301$
$=69.3 \mathrm{~s}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Half-life of a first order reaction is 20 seconds and initial concentration of reactant is 0.2 M. The concentration of reactant left after 80 seconds is
explanation:
${\rm{k}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{20}}$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{R}]} ;$
$\dfrac{0.693}{20}=\dfrac{2.303}{80} \log \dfrac{0.2}{[\mathrm{R}]}$
$1.2 = \log \frac{{0.2}}{{[{\rm{R}}]}}\,\,;\quad \,\,\,[{\rm{R}}] = 0.0125\,{\rm{M}}$
or ${{\rm{t}}_{1/2}} = 20\sec \,;\,\,\,\,{\rm{n}} = \frac{{80}}{{20}} = 4$
$0.2 \xrightarrow{20 \mathrm{sec}} 0.1 \xrightarrow{20 \mathrm{sec}} 0.05 \xrightarrow{20 \mathrm{sec}}$
$0.025 \xrightarrow{20 \mathrm{sec}} 0.0125$
subject: Chemistry

question: The initial concentrations of both the reactants of a second order reaction are equal and ${\mathrm{60 \%}}$ of the reaction gets completed in 30 s . How much time will be taken in ${\mathrm{20 \%}}$ completion of the reaction?
explanation:
For second order:
$\begin{align*}k_{2} & =\dfrac{1}{t} \cdot \dfrac{x}{a(a-x)} \\& =\dfrac{1}{30 \mathrm{~s}} \times \dfrac{0.6}{1(1-0.6)}=\dfrac{1}{30} \times \dfrac{0.6}{0.4}\end{align*}$
Now for ${\mathrm{20 \%}}$ completion,
${\mathrm{k_{2}=\dfrac{1}{t} \cdot \dfrac{x}{a(1-x)}}}$
(Since ${\mathrm{k_{2}}}$ is constant)
${\mathrm{\dfrac{1}{30} \times \dfrac{0.6}{0.4}=\dfrac{1}{t} \times \dfrac{1}{4}}}$
${\mathrm{t=\dfrac{30}{0.6} \times \dfrac{0.4}{4}=5 \mathrm{~s}}}$
subject: Chemistry

explanation:
For zero order reaction:
${\text{x}} = {\text{k}} \times {\text{t}}$
$\frac{{\text{a}}}{2} = {\text{k}} \times {{\text{t}}_{1/2}}$
When initial concentration is reduced to half-life will also reduce to half.

question: The ratio of the rate at the start of the reaction if half life $\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right)$ of the first order reaction and half life $\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right)$ of the second order reaction are equal is ____.
explanation:
For ${\text{I}}$ order. ${{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{[{{\text{t}}_{1/2}}]}}$;
For ${\text{ Il}}$ order. $\mathrm{k}_{2}=\dfrac{1}{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{a}}$
If ${\left( {{{\text{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)_1} = {\left( {{{\text{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)_2}$ then $\frac{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}}}} = 0.693{\text{a}}$
Initially $\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {{\text{r}}_1} = {{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}{[{\text{a}}]^1},\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}} = {{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}}{[{\text{a}}]^2}$
$\therefore \frac{{{{\text{r}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}}} = \frac{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{a}}}} = \frac{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}} \times 0.693 \times {\text{a}}}}{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}} \times {\text{a}}}} = 0.693$
subject: Chemistry

question: If the initial concentration of 0.6 M and the rate constant is ${\mathrm{2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}}$, the half-life of the reaction is ____ minutes.
explanation:
Since the unit of rate constant is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{M} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}}$, the reaction is zero order. Hence,
${\mathrm{t_{1/2}=\dfrac{[A]_{0}}{2 k}}}$
$=\dfrac{0.6}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}=1500 \mathrm{~s}=\dfrac{1500}{60}=25 \mathrm{~min}$

question:
Consider a first order gas phase decomposition reaction given below:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$
The initial pressure is 6.0 atm . The pressure drops to 3.0 atm after 6.93 min . How much time (in minutes) would it take to lower the partial pressure of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$ by 4.0 atm ?
[Consider: ${\mathrm{\log _{10}(3)=0.48}}$ ]
explanation:
Since the partial pressure reduces to half after 6.93 minutes, the half-life, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=6.93 \mathrm{~min}}}$
${\mathrm{k=\dfrac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{0.693}{6.93}=0.10 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}}}$
For a gas phase first order reaction,${\mathrm{k=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log _{10}\left(\dfrac{p_{i}}{p_{A}}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{i}}=6.0 \mathrm{~atm} ; \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.0-4.0=2.0 \mathrm{~atm}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 0.10=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log _{10}\left(\dfrac{6.0}{2.0}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad t=23.03 \log (3.0)=11.05 \mathrm{~min}}}$
$ \approx 11\,\min $
subject: Chemistry

question: Identify the formula which is applicable to the conversion of $20 \%$ of the initial concentration of the reactant to the product in a first order reaction. (Rate constant = k )
explanation:
For a first order reaction,
${\text{t}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{\text{k}}}{\text{log}}\frac{{\text{a}}}{{{\text{a}} - {\text{x}}}}$
${\text{t}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{\text{k}}}{\text{log}}\frac{{\text{a}}}{{{\text{a}} - {\text{a}}}}$
${{\text{t}}_{{\text{20}}\,\,\% }} = \frac{{2.303}}{{\text{k}}}{\text{log}}\frac{{\text{a}}}{{{\text{a}} - \frac{{\text{a}}}{5}}}$${\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left\{ {\because {\text{x}} = 20\,\% {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{a}}} \right\}$
$\therefore {{\text{t}}_{{\text{20}}\,\% }} = \frac{{2.303}}{{\text{k}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{log}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{5}{4}$

question:
For a reaction,
${\text{X(g)}} \to {\text{Y(g)}} + {\text{Z(g)}}$
the half-life period is $10 \mathrm{~min}$. In what period of time would the concentration of ${\text{X}}$ be reduced to $10 \%$ of original concentration?
explanation:
${\text{X(g)}} \to {\text{Y(g)}} + {\text{Z(g)}}$
The reaction is a first order reaction.
Hence,
${\text{k}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}}}} = \frac{{{\text{2}}{\text{.303}}}}{{\text{t}}}\log \frac{{\text{a}}}{{({\text{a}} - {\text{x}})}}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{0.693}}{{10}} = \frac{{{\text{2}}{\text{.303}}}}{{\text{t}}}\log \frac{{\text{a}}}{{{\text{a}}/10}}{\text{ }}$
${\text{or}}\,{\text{ t}} = \frac{{2.303 \times 10}}{{0.693}} \times \log 10 = 33\,{\text{min}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[\because \log 10 = 1]$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Consider the following statements:
The rate law for the acid catalysed hydrolysis of an ester being given as
Rate $ = {\text{k}}\left[ {{{\text{H}}^ + }} \right][{\text{ester}}] = {{\text{k}}^\prime }[{\text{ester}}]$. If the acid concentration is doubled at constant ester concentration
I. The second order rate constant, k is doubled.
II. The pesudo first order rate constant,${\text{k}}$ is doubled.
III. The rate of the reaction is doubled.
Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation: Pseudo first order rate constant is doubled as well as rate of reaction is doubled. It may be noted that in presence of acid, hydrolysis of ethyl acetate is a pseudo-unimolecular reaction but the actual value of ${\text{k}}$ depends upon the concentration of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions, otherwise rate constant of a reaction is constant at constant temperature.Thus, 'II' and 'III' are the correct statements.
subject: Chemistry

question: For a first order reaction, the time required for $99.9 \%$ of the reaction to take place is nearly
explanation:
Time required to complete $99.9 \%$ of reaction is given as:
${{\text{t}}_{{\text{99}}{\text{.9}}}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{\text{k}}}\log \frac{{100}}{{100 - 99.9}}$
$ = \frac{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.303}}}}{{\rm{k}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{log}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} 1000 = \frac{{{\rm{6}}{\rm{.909}}}}{{\rm{k}}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because \log {{10}^3} = 3} \right)$
${\text{and }}{{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}} = \frac{{{\text{2}}{\text{.303}}}}{{\text{k}}}\log \frac{{100}}{{100 - 50}}\quad $
$ = \frac{{{\text{2}}{\text{.303}}}}{{\text{k}}}\log 2 = \frac{{{\text{0}}{\text{.693}}}}{{\text{k}}}$
So, ${{\text{t}}_{{\text{99}}{\text{.9}}}}$ is 10 times that required for half of the reaction.

options.text: 10 times that required for half of the reaction
100 times that required for two-thirds of the reaction
10 times that required for one-fourth of the reaction
20 times that required for half of the reaction

subject: Chemistry

subject: Chemistry

question: Half-life of a reaction is found to be inversely proportional to the cube of initial concentration. The order of reaction is
explanation:
If ${{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}} \propto \frac{1}{{{{\text{a}}^{n - 1}}}}$, then
order of reaction $ = {\text{n}}$
Given that,${{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}} \propto \frac{1}{{{{\text{a}}^3}}}$
${\text{n}} - {\text{1 = 3}}$
$\therefore$ Order of reaction ${\text{(n)}} = 3 + 1 = 4$
subject: Chemistry

question: For reaction, $2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$, the rate is directly proportional to $\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]$. At $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 90 \%$ of the $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ reacts in 3600 seconds. The value of the rate constant is
explanation:
Reaction is of first order as given rate is proportional to $\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right.$ ]only.
So, $\quad \ln \left( {\frac{{\text{a}}}{{{\text{a}} - {\text{x}}}}} \right) = {\text{kt}}$
where, ${\text{a}} = $ initial concentration
${\text{a}} = 100{\text{ (Let)}}$
$ \Rightarrow ({\text{a}} - {\text{x}}) = 10\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(\because {\text{x}} = 90\% )$
${\text{a}} - {\text{x}} = $ concentration at an instant
${\rm{ln}}\left( {\frac{{100}}{{10}}} \right) = {\rm{k}} \times 3600\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} ({\rm{t}} = 3600\,{\rm{s}})$
$2.303\,\log 10 = {\text{k}} \times 3600\quad (\log 10 = 1)$
${\text{k}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{3600}} = 6.4 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Find out time period of ${{\rm{I}}^{{\rm{st}}}}$ order reaction when reaction complete $2/3\,{\rm{rd}}$, if the value of rate constant is $4.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
explanation:
For a first order reaction,
${\text{t}} = \frac{{{\text{2}}{\text{.303}}}}{{\text{k}}}\log \frac{{\text{a}}}{{{\text{a}} - {\text{x}}}}$
where, ${\text{a}} = $ initial concentration
${\text{a}} - {\text{x}} = $ final concentration
Given, rate constant ${\text{(k)}} = 4.3 \times {10^{ - 4}}{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$
Let, the initial concentration $ = {\text{a}}$
$\therefore$ Final concentration $({\text{a}} - {\text{x}})$
$ = {\text{a}} - \frac{2}{3}{\text{a}} = \frac{{\text{a}}}{{\text{3}}}$
For first order reaction,
${\text{t}} = \frac{{{\text{2}}{\text{.303}}}}{{\text{k}}}\log \frac{{\text{a}}}{{{\text{a}} - {\text{x}}}}$
$ = \frac{{2.303}}{{4.3 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}\log \frac{{\text{a}}}{{{\text{a/3}}}}$
$ = 2.5 \times {10^3}{\text{sec}}$
Thus, option (4) is correct.
subject: Chemistry

question: If half-life of a substance is 36 minutes. Find amount left after 2 hrs. Initial amount is $10 \mathrm{~g}$
explanation:
Given, ${{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}} = 36\,{\text{min}}$
${\text{t}} = 2{\text{hrs}} = 120\,{\text{min}},\left[ {{{\text{A}}_0}} \right] = 10\,{\text{g}}$
For first order reaction, ${\text{k}} = \frac{{{\text{0}}{\text{.693}}}}{{{{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}}}}$
$\therefore \quad {\text{k}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{36}} = 0.01925$
${\text{t}} = \frac{{{\text{2}}{\text{.303}}}}{{\text{k}}}\log \frac{{{{{\text{[A]}}}_{\text{0}}}}}{{{\text{[A]}}}}{\text{g}}$
where, ${[{\text{A]}}_0} = $ initial amount $=10 \mathrm{~g}$
${\text{[A] = }}$ final amount
$\therefore \quad 120 = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.01925}}\log \frac{{10}}{{[{\text{A}}]}}$
$\log 10 - \log [{\text{A}}] = 1$
$1 - \log [{\text{A}}] = 1$
$\log [{\text{A}}] = 0$
$[{\text{A}}] = 1\,{\text{g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Formula for half- life of a zero order reaction is
explanation:
For zero order reaction, if concentration of product is $\left[ {{{\text{C}}_0}} \right]$ at time ${{\text{t}}_{\text{1}}}$ and $\frac{{\left[ {{{\text{C}}_0}} \right]}}{2}$ at time ${{\text{t}}_{\text{2}}}$, then
${\text{k}} = \frac{{\left[ {\frac{{{{\text{C}}_{\text{0}}}}}{{\text{2}}}} \right] - \left[ {{{\text{C}}_0}} \right]}}{{{{\text{t}}_2} - {{\text{t}}_1}}} = \frac{{\left[ {{{\text{C}}_0}} \right] - \left[ {\frac{{{{\text{C}}_{\text{0}}}}}{{\text{2}}}} \right]}}{{{{\text{t}}_{1/2}}}}$
$\therefore {{\text{t}}_{1/2}} = \frac{{\left[ {{{\text{C}}_{\text{0}}}} \right]}}{{{\text{2k}}}}$
Thus,${{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}}$ for zero order reaction is directly proportional to the initial concentration of the reactant.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Consider the following statement in respect of zero order reaction.
I. The rate of the reaction is independent of reactant concentration.
II. The rate of the reaction is independent of temperature.
III. Rate law expression: $\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{0}$ and unit of $\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{s}^{-1}$
IV. The rate constant of reaction is independent of reactant concentration.
Choose the correct statements/s
explanation: For a zero-order reaction, rate and rate constant are independent of reactant concentration. Units of K are mol. $1 \mathrm{lit}^{-} \mathrm{sec}^{-}$.In general, rate increases with increase in temp.
subject: Chemistry

question: Under the same reaction conditions, initial concentration of  $1.386\,{\text{mol}}\,\,{\text{d}}{{\text{m}}^{ - 3}}$  of a substance becomes half in 40 seconds and 20 seconds through first order and zero order kinetics, respectively. Ratio $\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{\mathrm{k}_{0}}\right)$ of rate constant for first order $\left(k_{1}\right)$ and zero order $\left(k_{0}\right)$ of the reaction is
explanation:
${\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)_1} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{1}}}}} = 40\,\,{\rm{sec}}$
${\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)_0} = \frac{{{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{0}}}}}{{2{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{0}}}}} = 20\,\,{\rm{sec}}$
given, $\mathrm{a}_{0}=1.386$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{1}}{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{0}}=\dfrac{0.693 \times 2 \mathrm{k}_{0}}{\mathrm{k}_{1} \times 1.386}=\dfrac{40}{20}$
on solving
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{\mathrm{k}_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Match the Column I with Column II and mark the appropriate choice.
explanation:
Zero order reaction, ${\rm{x}} = {\rm{k t}},$ units of
$\mathrm{k}=$ mol. lit ${ }^{-} \mathrm{sec}^{-}$
For second order reaction,
$2 \mathrm{~A} \rightarrow$ Products
${\rm{K}} = \frac{1}{{\rm{t}}}\left[ {\frac{1}{{({\rm{a}} - {\rm{x}})}} - \frac{1}{{\rm{a}}}} \right]$
units of $\mathrm{K}=$ lit. $\mathrm{mol}^{-} \mathrm{sec}^{-}$or $\mathrm{atm}^{-} \mathrm{sec}^{-}$
For $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow$ Products
${\rm{K}} = \frac{{2 \cdot 303}}{{{\rm{t}}({\rm{a}} - {\rm{b}})}}{\rm{log}}\left[ {\frac{{{\rm{b}}({\rm{a}} - {\rm{x}})}}{{{\rm{a}}({\rm{b}} - {\rm{x}})}}} \right]$
units of $\mathrm{K}=$ lit. $\mathrm{mol}^{-} \mathrm{sec}^{-}$or $\mathrm{atm}^{-} \mathrm{sec}^{-}$
For 1st order reaction, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ Products
units of $\mathrm{K}=\mathrm{sec}^{-}$.
$\mathrm{K}=\dfrac{2 \cdot 303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})}$

options.text: A - P,	B - Q,R,	C - Q,	D - S

question: Match the Column I with Column II and mark the appropriate choice.
explanation:
For zero order, ${{\rm{t}}_{1/2}} \propto {\rm{a}}$ $({\rm{a}} \to {\rm{Initial}}\,\,{\rm{conc}}.)$
$\text { For } 1^{\text {st }} \text { order, } \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \propto \mathrm{a}^{0} \text { and } \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{~K}}$
For $2^{\text {nd }}$ order, $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \propto \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{a}}$
$\text { For } 3^{\text {rd }} \text { order, } \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \propto \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}$

options.text: A - R,	B - P,	C - S,		D - Q

question:
Select the correct statements out of ${\rm{P, Q}}$ and ${\rm{R}}$ for a zero order reaction.
(P) Quantity of the product formed is directly proportional to time
(Q) Larger the initial concentration of the reactant, greater the half-life period
(R) If $50 \%$ reaction takes place in 100 minutes, $75 \%$ reaction takes place in 150 minutes.
explanation:
For zero order reaction,
(i) $[$ Product $]=\mathrm{kt} \Rightarrow[$ Product $] \propto \mathrm{t}$
(ii) $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{\mathrm{A}_{0}}{2 \mathrm{k}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \propto\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]$
(iii) Equal percentage of reaction occurs in equal time intervals.
If $50 \%$ occurs in 100 minutes then $25 \%$ occurs in 50 minutes.
$\Rightarrow 75 \%$ reaction occurs in $100+50=150 \mathrm{~min}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: In the following first order reactions, $({\text{A}})\xrightarrow{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}}}$ product, $({\text{B}})\xrightarrow{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}}}}$ product, the ratio $\mathrm{k}_{1} / \mathrm{k}_{2}$ if $90 \%$ of (A) has been reacted in time ' $t$ ' while $99 \%$ of (B) has been reacted in time $2 t$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{k}_{1}=\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \ln \left(\dfrac{\mathrm{A}_{0}}{0.1 \mathrm{~A}_{0}}\right)$
$\mathrm{k}_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2 \mathrm{t}} \ln \left(\dfrac{\mathrm{B}_{0}}{0.01 \mathrm{~B}_{0}}\right)$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{\mathrm{k}_{2}}=2 \dfrac{\ln (10)}{\ln (100)}=\dfrac{2 \ln (10)}{\ln (10)^{2}}=\dfrac{2 \ln (10)}{2 \ln (10)}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{\mathrm{k}_{2}}=1$
subject: Chemistry

explanation: The hydrolysis of cane sugar is considered as a pseudo-first-order reaction due to the excess and constant concentration of water. Hence, option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Hydrolysis of ethyl acetate in presence of acid is a reaction of first order whereas in presence of alkali, it is a reaction of second order.


Reason: Acid only acts as a catalyst whereas alkali acts as one of the reactants.
explanation:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$
Rate $\propto\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right]$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$
Rate $\propto\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right][\mathrm{NaOH}]$
Thus option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: The decomposition of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ on finely divided platinum surface is first order when the concentration is low, however at higher concentration, the reaction becomes zero order.

Reason: In first order reaction, the rate of reaction is proportional to the first power of the concentration of the reactant.
explanation: In heterogeneous systems, the reaction rate initially follows first-order kinetics proportional to the reactant concentration. However, at higher concentrations, it becomes a zero-order reaction, where the rate depends solely on the availability of active sites on the catalyst surface. Given reason statement is true, but not the correct explantion for the assertion. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question: $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ is a first order reaction. The initial concentration of ${\text{A}}$ is $0.2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. After $10 \mathrm{~min}$, the concentration of ${\text{B}}$ is found to be $0.18 \mathrm{~mol}$ $\mathrm{L}^{-1}$. The rate constant (in $\min ^{-1}$ ) for the reaction is:
explanation:
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})}$
$\mathrm{a}=0.2 ;(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})=0.02$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{10} \log \dfrac{0.2}{0.02}=\dfrac{2.303}{10} \log 10=0.2303$

question: For the first order reaction half-life is $14 \mathrm{sec}$, the time required for the initial concentration to reduce to $1 / 8$ of its value is:
explanation:
${{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}}{\text{ = 14sec, N = }}\frac{{{{\text{N}}_{\text{0}}}}}{{\text{8}}}$
$\frac{{\text{N}}}{{{{\text{N}}_{\text{o}}}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^{\text{n}}}$
$\frac{{\text{N}}}{{8\,{{\text{N}}_{\text{o}}}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^{\text{n}}} \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^{\text{n}}} \Rightarrow {\text{n}} = 3$
$\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{nt}_{1 / 2}=3 \times 14=42 \mathrm{sec}$
(or) $1-\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{1}{4}-\dfrac{1}{8} ; 3$ half-lives $3 \times 14 = 42\,\,{\text{sec}}$

question: What is rate constant of a first order reaction if 0.08 mole of reactant reduces to 0.02 mole in 23.03 minute?
explanation:
For first order reaction,
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log _{10} \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{23.03 \min } \log _{10} \dfrac{0.08}{0.02}$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{23.03 \min } \log _{10} 4=0.1 \mathrm{~min}^{-1} \times 0.6021$
$\therefore \mathrm{k}=0.06021 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For a reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ product, rate constant is $2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The initial concentration of $\mathrm{A}$ is ${\text{1}}{\text{.0 mol d}}{{\text{m}}^{{\text{ - 3}}}}$. What is the value of $\log \dfrac{1}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}$ after 100 seconds?
explanation:
${\text{k = 2}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 2}}}}{\text{}}{{\text{s}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}{\text{,[ A}}{{\text{]}}_{\text{0}}}{\text{ = 1}}{\text{.0 mol d}}{{\text{m}}^{{\text{ - 3}}}}$
$\mathrm{t}=100 \mathrm{~s}, \log \dfrac{1}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}=$ ?
For first order reaction,
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log _{10} \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}$
$\therefore \log _{10} \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{k} \times \mathrm{t}}{2.303}$
$\therefore {\log _{10}}\frac{1}{{{{[\,{\text{A}}]}_{\text{t}}}}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 100}}{{2.303}} = 0.868\,\,{\text{mol d}}{{\text{m}}^{{\text{ - 3}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the half - life of a first order reaction if time required to decrease concentration of reactant from $1.0 \mathrm{M}$ to $0.25 \mathrm{M}$ is 10 hour?
explanation:
$\mathrm{t}=10$ hour, $[\mathrm{A}]_{0}=1.0 \mathrm{M},[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=0.25 \mathrm{M}$ For first order reaction,
(i) $\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log _{10} \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}=\dfrac{2.303}{10 \mathrm{~h}} \log _{10} \dfrac{1.0}{0.25}$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{10 \mathrm{~h}} \log _{10} 4 \quad=\dfrac{2.303 \times 0.6020}{10 \mathrm{~h}}$
$=0.139 \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$
(ii) $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}=\dfrac{0.693}{0.139 \mathrm{~h}^{-1}}$
$=4.98$ hour $\approx 5$ hours
subject: Chemistry

question: For a first order reaction, intercept of the graph between $\log \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{1}}$ (Y-axis) and conc (X-axis) is equal to
explanation: It starts from origin, so intercept is zero.

question: In a first order reaction, concentration of reactant is reduced to $(1 / 8)^{\text {th }}$ of concentration in 23.03 minutes. What is half-life period of reaction?
explanation:
$[\mathrm{A}]_{0}=1,[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=\dfrac{1}{8}, \mathrm{t}=23.03 \mathrm{~min}$
For first order reaction,
(i) $\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log _{10} \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{23.03} \log _{10} \dfrac{1}{(1 / 8)}=0.1 \times \log 8$
$=0.1 \times 0.9030$
$=0.0903 \mathrm{~min}^{-}$
(ii) $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}=\dfrac{0.693}{0.0903 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}}=7.7 \mathrm{~min}$

$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{1.151 \mathrm{~min}} \log _{10}\left(\dfrac{20}{10}\right)=\dfrac{2.303 \times \log 2}{1.151} \\& =\dfrac{2.303 \times 0.3010}{1.151}=0.60 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

question: Half life for a first order reaction is 6.93 hour. What is the time required for $80 \%$ completion of the reaction?

Here, $[\mathrm{A}]_{0}=100,[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=100-80=20$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log _{10} \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{0.1} \log _{10} \dfrac{100}{20}=23.03 \times \log _{10} 5$
$=23.03 \times 0.699=16.10$ hours

explanation:
By using first order integrated rate equation we can find the percentage of A remaining $\left( {\frac{{{\rm{a - x}}}}{{\rm{a}}}} \right)$ after ${\rm{60\,min}}$.
${\rm{log}}\left( {\frac{{\rm{a}}}{{{\rm{a - x}}}}} \right){\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{kt}}}}{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.303}}}}$
${\rm{log}}\left( {\frac{{\rm{a}}}{{{\rm{a - x}}}}} \right){\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{3}}{\rm{.465 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 2}}}}{\rm{ \times 60}}}}{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.303}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.9}}$
$\frac{{\rm{a}}}{{{\rm{a - x}}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.9}}}}{\rm{ = 8}}$
$\therefore \,\frac{{{\rm{a - x}}}}{{\rm{a}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{8}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.125}}$

explanation:
