$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{t}{50}=\dfrac{4 \ln 10}{3 \ln 10} \\& t=\dfrac{4 \times 50}{3}=66.7 \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The reaction ${\rm{A}} \to {\rm{B}}$ follows first order kinetics. The time taken for 0.8 mole of A to produce 0.6 mole of B is 1 hour. What is the time taken for conversion of 0.9 mole of A to produce 0.675 mole of $\mathrm{B}$ ?
explanation:
Given, $a=0.8 \mathrm{~mol},(a-x)=0.8-0.6=0.2$
$k=\dfrac{2.303}{1} \log \dfrac{0.8}{0.2} \text { or } k=2.303 \log 4$
Again, $a=0.9, \quad a-x=0.9-0.675=0.225$
$\begin{aligned}& k=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log \dfrac{0.9}{0.225} \\& 2.303 \log 4=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log 4\end{aligned}$
Hence, $\mathrm{t}=1$ hour
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following represents the expression for $\dfrac{4^{\text {th }}}{6}$ life of a first order reaction?
explanation:
$t=\left(\dfrac{2.303}{k}\right) \log \left(\dfrac{a}{a-x}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}& t=\left(\dfrac{2.303}{k}\right) \log \left[\dfrac{1}{1-\left(\dfrac{4}{6}\right)}\right] \\& t=\left(\dfrac{2.303}{k}\right) \log 3\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For the first order reaction ${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{O(g)}} \to {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{(g) + CO(g)}}$, the initial pressure of ${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{O(g)}}$ is 80 torr and total pressure at the end of 20 minutes is 120 torr. The time needed for $75 \%$ decomposition of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}$ would be:
explanation:
Let $x$ torr of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}$ decompose after 20 $\min$. Then, $80-x+2 x=120 ; x=40$ torr $=50 \%$ of initial pressure.
Hence, $t_{1 / 2}=20 \mathrm{~min}$. For $75 \%$ reaction, dfraction left $=\dfrac{25}{100}=\dfrac{1}{4}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2}$
No. of half lives $=2$. Time needed for $75 \%$ reaction $=2 \times 20=40 \mathrm{~min}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A first order reaction has a specific reaction rate of ${\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 2}}}}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$. How much time it take for ${\rm{20\,g}}$ of the reactant to reduce to ${\rm{5\,g}}$ ?
explanation:
Half life of first order reaction,
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}=\dfrac{0.693}{10^{-2}}=69.3 \mathrm{sec}$
Method - 1 https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764948834/preptracker/questions/q_23468_explanation_1764948834247.png Total time $=2 \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=2 \times 69.3=138.6 \mathrm{sec}$
Method - 2
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}} \log \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{o}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}} \\& \mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{10^{-2}} \log \dfrac{20}{5} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=138.6 \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{P}$, is of zero order with rate constant $\mathrm{k}$ while the reaction $\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{P}$, is first order reaction with rate constant $\mathrm{k}$. For what initial concentration of A are the half lives of the two reactions equal?
explanation:
For a zero order reaction, $t_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{a}{2 k}$ and for first order reaction, $t_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$
The two half lives will be equal when $a=2 \ln 2$ or $\ln 4$
subject: Chemistry

question: The ${{\rm{t}}_{{\rm{1/2}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{U}}^{{\rm{234is}}}}{\rm{2}}{\rm{.5 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{5}}}$ years. In how much time is the quantity of the isotope reduced to 25% of the original amount?
explanation:
$\begin{aligned}& N=\dfrac{N_{0}}{2^{n}} \\& 25=\dfrac{100}{2^{n}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}n & =2 \\T & =n \times t_{1 / 2} \\& =2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{5} \\& =5 \times 10^{5}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For a first order reaction, the half-life period is
explanation:
$t_{\dfrac{1}{2}}=0.693 k$
$t_{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ of first order reaction is independent of initial concentration of the reactant.

options.text: Inversely related to the square of initial concentration

subject: Chemistry

question: 75% of a first order reaction was completed in 32 minute; when was 50% of the reaction completed?
explanation: $\begin{aligned}\mathrm{k}_{75 \%} & =\left(\dfrac{2.303}{32}\right) \log (4)=0.043 \\\mathrm{t}_{50 \%} & =0.693 / \mathrm{k}=16 \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For a first order reaction, the rate constant is 1 $\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M} / \mathrm{s}$ and the initial concentration of the reactant is $1 \mathrm{M}$. The half life period for the reaction is
explanation:
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{\text { rate }}{[A]}=10^{-2}$
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{k}=\dfrac{0.693}{10^{-2}}=69.3 \mathrm{sec}$
subject: Chemistry

question: ${\rm{Cu - 64}}$ isotope is used in scans of brain tumour. Assuming no loss of ${\rm{Cu - 64}}$ except by radioactive decay, how many hours are required for an administrated dose of ${\rm{Cu - 64}}$ to drop to    3.125% of the initial injected into a human $\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{{\rm{1/2}}}}\,\,{\rm{for}}\,\,{\rm{Cu}}\,\,{\rm{64 = 12}}{\rm{.8}}\,{\rm{hrs}}} \right)$
explanation:
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=12.8 \mathrm{hrs}$
$3.125 \% \text { remaining }=5 \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5 \times 12.8=$$64 \mathrm{hrs}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate equation for a reaction, ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}} \to {{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}\,\,{\rm{is}}\,\,{\rm{rate = k}}{\left[ {{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}} \right]^{\rm{0}}}{\rm{ = k}}$. If the initial concentration of the reactant is "a" mol ${\rm{Li}}{{\rm{t}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$, the half-life period of the reaction is
explanation:
For a zero order reaction
${{\rm{t}}_{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{a}}}{{{\rm{2k}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Consider the following in respect of $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction
I. ${{\rm{t}}_{{\rm{1/2}}}}$ is independent of the initial concentration
II. Time taken for the completion of the reaction is twice its ${{\rm{t}}_{{\rm{1/2}}}}$
III. Concentration of the reactant decreases exponentially with time
Which of the statements given above are correct?
subject: Chemistry

question: The hydrolysis ethyl acetate is represented by $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOEt}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{EtOH}$ It is a reaction of
explanation: Hydrolysis of ester is an example of pseudo unimolecular reaction.
subject: Chemistry

question: Decomposition of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ follows a first order reaction. In fifty minutes the concentration of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ decreases from 0.5 to $0.125 \mathrm{M}$ in one such decomposition. When the concentration of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ reaches $0.05 \mathrm{M}$, the rate of formation of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ will be :
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& 2 t_{1 / 2}=50 \mathrm{~min} \\& t_{1 / 2}=25 \mathrm{~min} \\& \therefore k=\dfrac{0.693}{25} \\& -\dfrac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} O_{2}\right]}{d t}=\dfrac{d\left[O_{2}\right]}{d t} \times 2 \\& \therefore \dfrac{d\left[O_{2}\right]}{d t}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{0.693}{25} \times 0.05 \\& =6.93 \times 10^{-4}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For a homogeneous gaseous reaction ${\rm{A}} \to {\rm{B}} + {\rm{C}} + {\rm{D}}$, the initial pressure was ${{\rm{P}}_{\rm{0}}}$ while pressure after time 't' was P if $\left( {{\rm{P > }}{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{0}}}} \right)$. The expression for the rate constant k is
explanation:
Given reaction is
$\begin{array}{lllll} & \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D} \\\text { At } \mathrm{t}=0 & \mathrm{P}_{0} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\\text { At } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t} & \mathrm{P}_{0}-\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x}\end{array}$
At time t, pressure is $\mathrm{P}$
$\begin{aligned}& P_{0}-x+x+x+x=P \\& x=\dfrac{P-P_{0}}{2}\end{aligned}$
For first order reaction,
$k=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log \left[\dfrac{P_{0}}{P_{0}-x}\right]$
$k=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log \left[\dfrac{P_{0}}{P_{0}\left(\dfrac{P-P_{0}}{2}\right)}\right]=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log \left[\dfrac{2 P_{0}}{3 P_{0}-P}\right]$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Consider the following first order competing reactions:
${\text{X}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}}}{\text{A + B and Y}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{C + D}}$
If 50 % of the reaction of X was completed when 96 % of the reaction of Y was completed, the ratio of their rate constants $\left( {{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{/}}{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right)$ is
explanation:
$\dfrac{0.693}{k_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{2.303}{k_{2}}\right) \log \left(\dfrac{100}{4}\right)$
on solving, $\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=4.65$
subject: Chemistry

question: If the rate constant for the disintegration of a radiactive nucleus is $\lambda$. Therefore, the probability $\mathrm{P}$ of survival of a radiactive nucleus for one mean life is
explanation:
$\mathrm{P}_{\text {survival }}=\dfrac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda t}}{\mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{o}}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\lambda t}$
For, mean life $=\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ $P_{\text {survival }}=e^{-\lambda \times \dfrac{1}{\lambda}}=e^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A drop of solution (volume $0.05 \mathrm{~mL}$ ) contains \${\rm{3}}{\rm{.0 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 6}}}}$ moles of ${{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}$. If the rate constant of disappearances of ${{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}$ is ${\rm{1}}{\rm{.0 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 7}}}}$ mole litre $^{{\rm{ - 1}}}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$. How long would it take for ${{\text{H}}^{\text{ + }}}$ in drop to disappear?
explanation:
Units of rate constant ${\rm{ = mol}}\,{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ i.e., zero order reaction
$\begin{aligned}{\left[A_{0}\right]-[A] } & =k t\left[A_{0}\right]=\dfrac{3 \times 10^{-6} \times 100}{0.05 \times 10^{-3}}=\dfrac{3}{50} \\\Rightarrow \quad t & =\dfrac{3}{50} \times \dfrac{10^{-7}}{1} \\& =6 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Number of average life times $\left( {{{\rm{T}}_{{\rm{av}}}}} \right)$ required for a first order reaction to achieve 99.9% level of completion is
explanation:
Time required for $99.9 \%$ reaction is 10 times of half-life period.
$t_{99.9}=10 t_{50}$
But $t_{50}=\dfrac{0.693}{k}$
$\therefore \quad T_{a v}=\dfrac{1}{k}$
and $t_{50}=0.693 T_{a v}$
$\therefore \quad t_{99.9}=10 \times 0.693 T_{a v}=6.93 T_{a v}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For a first order reaction ${\rm{A}} \to {\rm{B}}$ the reaction rate at which reactant concentration of 0.01 M is found to be ${\rm{2}}{\rm{.0 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 5}}}}{\rm{M}}\,{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$. The half -life period of the reaction is :
explanation:
rate $=k[\mathrm{~A}]^{1}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01}=2.0 \times 10^{-3} \\& \therefore \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}=347 \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For the first order reaction, half-life is 14s. The time required for the initial concentration to reduce to ${\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{8}}}^{{\text{th}}}}$ of its value is
explanation:
For initial conc. to reduce to ${\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{8}}}^{{\text{th}}}}$, no. of half lives required is 3 .
$\therefore $ Total time ${\rm{ = 3 \times 14 = 42}}\,\,{\rm{sec}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate equation for the reaction ${\text{2A + B}} \to {\text{C}}$ is found to be: rate${\text{  = k[A][B]}}$. The correct statement in relation to this reaction is
explanation:
The overall order of the reaction is 2 .
(1) Unit of $\mathrm{k}$ is $\mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^{1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ (2) $t_{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ of second order reaction is inversely proportional to the initial concentration
(3) Rate of formation of $\mathrm{C}$ is half of the rate of disappearance of A.
(4) Rate constant vary with temperature

options.text: Rate of formation of $\mathrm{C}$ is twice the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{A}$
Value of $\mathrm{k}$ is independent of the initial concentrations of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$.

subject: Chemistry

question:
At high pressure, the following reaction is of zero order.
${\text{2N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g)}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{Platinum catalyst}}}]{{{\text{1130K}}}}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + 3}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}$
Which of the following statements are correct for above reaction?
(i) Rate of reaction = Rate constant
(ii) Rate of reaction depends on concentration of ammonia.
(iii) Rate of decomposition of ammonia will remain constant until ammonia disappears completely.
(iv) Further increase in pressure will change the rate of reaction.
explanation: Conceptual
subject: Chemistry

question: The reaction ; ${\text{A}} \to {\text{B}}$ follows first order kinetics. The time taken for 0.8 mole of A to produce 0.6 mole of $\mathrm{B}$ is 1 hour. What is the time taken for conversion of 0.9 mole of ${\text{A}}$ to produce 0.675 mole of $\mathrm{B}$ ?
explanation:
In case of first order reactions half-life and any dfraction of reaction is independent of initial concentration of reactants.
Alternately,
or
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})} \\& \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{0.8}{(0.8-0.6)}=1.386\end{aligned}$
Also, $1.386=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{0.9}{(0.9-0.675)}=1 \mathrm{hr}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Two substances ${\text{A and B}}$ are present such that${{\text{[A]}}_{\text{0}}}{\text{ = 4[B}}{{\text{]}}_{\text{0}}}$ and half life of A is 5 minute and that of B is 15 minute. If they start decaying at the same time following first order kinetics how much time will the concentration of both of them would be the same?
explanation:
Give, $[A]_{0}=4[B]_{0}$
$t_{1 / 2}$ of $\mathrm{A}=5 \mathrm{~min}$
Rate of constant of $\mathrm{A}, k_{A}=\dfrac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{0.693}{5}$
$t_{1 / 2}$ of $\mathrm{B}=15 \mathrm{~min}$
Rate of constant of $\mathrm{B}, k_{B}=\dfrac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{0.693}{15}$
And also given, $[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]$
$[A]_{0} e^{-k} A^{t}=[B]_{0} e^{-k B^{t}}$
$\dfrac{[A]_{0}}{[B]_{0}}=\dfrac{e^{-k_{B^{t}}}}{e^{-k_{A^{t}}}}=e^{\left(k_{A}-k_{B}\right) t}$ $4=e^{\left(\dfrac{0.693}{5}-\dfrac{0.693}{15}\right) t}$
In $4=\left(\dfrac{0.693}{5}-\dfrac{0.693}{15}\right) t$
Upon solving, we get
$\mathrm{t}=15 \mathrm{~min}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In a first order reaction, 75% of the reactant disappeared in 1.386 hours. The rate constant of the reaction is
subject: Chemistry

question: A reaction ${\text{2A   +   B}}\xrightarrow{{\text{K}}}{\text{C   +   D}}$ is first order with respect to A and second order with respect to B. Initial conc. ${\text{(t = 0) of}}\,{\text{ A }}\,{\text{is }}{{\text{C}}_{\text{0}}}$ while ${\rm{B}}\,\,{\rm{is}}\,\,{\rm{2}}\,{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{0}}}$ is ${\text{2}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{0}}}$. If at t as 30 minutes the conc. of ${\text{C}}\,\,{\text{is }}{{\text{C}}_{\text{0}}}{\text{/4}}$, then rate expression at ${\text{t = 30}}$ minutes is:
subject: Chemistry

question:
Thermal decomposition of compound ${\text{x}}$ is a first order reaction. If 75% of ${\text{x}}$ is decomposed in 100 min. How long will it take for 90% of the compound to decompose?
Given ${\text{log2 = 0}}{\text{.30}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For an irreversible process ${\rm{A + B}} \to $ products, the rate is first-order w.r.t. A and second-order w.r.t. B, if ${\rm{1}}{\rm{.0\,mol}}$ each of ${\rm{A}}$ and ${\rm{B}}$ introduced into a ${\rm{1}}{\rm{.0\,L}}$ vessel, and the initial rate was ${\rm{1}}{\rm{.0 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 2}}}}{\rm{\,mol\,}}{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{\,}}{{\rm{s}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$, rate when half reactants have been turned into products is
explanation:
$A+B \longrightarrow$ product
$\begin{aligned}& r=k[A]^{1}[B]^{2} \\& r_{1}=k[1]^{1}[1]^{2}=1 \times 10^{-2} \quad\left(k=1 \times 10^{-2}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following is not a first order of reaction?
explanation: Decomposition of HI on gold surface follows zero order kinetics

options.text: Natural radioactive decay of unstable nuclei

subject: Chemistry

question: The time for half-life period of a certain reaction, ${\text{A}} \to $ products is ${\text{1h}}$. When the initial concentration of the reactant ${\text{A}}$ is ${\text{2}}{\text{.0}}\,{\text{mol}}\,{{\text{L}}^{{\text{  -  1}}}}$, how much time does it take for its concentration to come from 0.50 to 0.25 ${\text{mol}}{{\text{L}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$, if it is a zero-order reaction?
explanation:
Half-life time for a zero order reaction is given by
${{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\left[ {{{\text{A}}_{\text{o}}}} \right]}}{{{\text{2k}}}}$
[where, $\left[A_{o}\right]=$ initial concentration of reactant $]$
$\Rightarrow \quad k_{o}=\dfrac{\left[A_{o}\right]}{2 t_{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{2.0 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}{2 \times 1 \mathrm{~h}}=1.0 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
Rate constant for a zero-order reaction is given by
${\text{k = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{t}}}\left[ {\left( {{{\text{A}}_{\text{o}}}} \right){\text{ - (A)}}} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{t = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{k}}}\left[ {\left( {{{\text{A}}_{\text{o}}}} \right){\text{ - (A)}}} \right]$
${\rm{t = }}\frac{{{\rm{(0}}{\rm{.50) - (0}}{\rm{.25)mol}}{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}}}{{{\rm{1}}\,{\rm{mol}}\,{{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{{\rm{h}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{t = 0}}{\text{.25h}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Consider the reaction: ${\rm{A}} \to {\rm{B + C}}$ Initial concentration of ${\rm{A}}$ is 1 M. 20 minutes time is required for completion of 20% reaction. If $\frac{{{\rm{d[B]}}}}{{{\rm{dt}}}}{\rm{ = k[A]}}$, then half life $\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{{\rm{1/2}}}}} \right)$ is
explanation:
Given differential rate law indicates that the reaction follows first order reaction.
$k=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log \dfrac{[A]_{o}}{[A]}=\dfrac{2.303}{20}$
$\log \left(\dfrac{1}{1-0.2}\right)=0.011 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
$t_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{k}=\dfrac{0.693}{0.011}=63 \mathrm{~min}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Half-life of a zero order reaction is${\rm{250}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{sec}}{\rm{.}}\,{{\rm{t}}_{{\rm{75\% }}}}{\rm{,}}\,{{\rm{t}}_{{\rm{100\% }}}}$ of the reaction respectively in sec are
subject: Chemistry

question: For a first-order reaction ${\text{(A)}} \to $ products, the concentration of A changes from${\text{0}}{\text{.1M}}$ to 0.025 M in${\text{40\,min}}$. The rate of reaction when the concentration of ${\text{A}}\,\,{\text{is 0}}{\text{.01M}}\,\,{\text{is}}$
explanation:
Rate constant for the first order reaction is given by
$\begin{aligned}& k=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log \left(\dfrac{a}{a-x}\right) \\& a=0.1 M \\&(a-x)=0.025 M \\& t=40 \mathrm{~min} \\& \therefore \quad k=\dfrac{2.303}{40} \log \dfrac{0.1}{0.025 M}=0.0347 \mathrm{~min}^{-1} \\& \text { Rate }=\left(\dfrac{d x}{d t}\right)=k[A]^{1}=0.0347 \times 0.01 \\&=3.47 \times 10^{-4} M \mathrm{~min}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which graph represents zero order reaction $[\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g})] ?$
explanation: In case of Zero order reaction, ${{\text{t}}_{\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}}}\,{\text{or }}{{\text{t}}_{\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{4}}}}}$ is directly proportional to the initial concentration of reactants.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following curve represent zero order reaction of $A \rightarrow$ products?
explanation:
For zero-order reaction
$\begin{gathered}\dfrac{-d[A]}{d t}=k \\\text { or }[A]_{0}-[A]=k t \\\text { or }[A]=\left[A_{0}\right]-k t\end{gathered}$
It means concentration of A linearly decreases with $t$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following curves represents a $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction?
explanation: Half life time of first order reaction is independent of initial concentration of reactant.
subject: Chemistry

question: The value of rate constant of a pseudo first order reaction is
explanation:
Pseudo first order reaction is a chemical reaction in which rate of reaction depends upon concentration of only one reactant while concentration of another reactant has no effect on rate of reaction.
e.g., hydrolysis of ethyl acetate in presence of excess of water $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\underset{\text { excess }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \stackrel{\mathrm{H}^{+}}{\longrightarrow}} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$
$r=\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]$
Excess $\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]$ can cause the independency of reaction on $\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]$.

options.text: Independent on the concentration of reactants present in small amount
Depends on the concentration of reactants present in excess
Is independent of the concentration of reactants

subject: Chemistry

question: A first order reaction is $50 \%$ completed in $1.26 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~s}$. How much time would it take for $100 \%$ completion?
explanation: It is impossible to perform $100 \%$ of the reaction. Whole of the substance never reacts because in every half-life, $50 \%$ of the substance reacts. Hence, time taken for $100 \%$ completion of a reaction is infinite.
subject: Chemistry

question: A radioactive isotope having a half-life period of 3 days was received after 12 days. If $3 \mathrm{~g}$ of the isotope is left in the container, what would be the initial mass of the isotope?
explanation:
Given $t_{1 / 2}=3$
Total time, $\mathrm{T}=12$
No. of half lives $(\mathrm{n})=\dfrac{12}{3}=4$
$\begin{aligned}& \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}} \\& \therefore\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{4}=\dfrac{3}{\mathrm{~N}}\end{aligned}$$\dfrac{3}{\mathrm{~N}}=\dfrac{1}{16}, \quad \mathrm{~N}=48 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The half life period of a first order reaction is 15 minutes. The amount of substance left after one hour will be :
explanation:
Since $t_{1 / 2}=15 \mathrm{~min}$
$\therefore \text { No. of half lives }=\dfrac{6}{15}=4$
$\therefore$ Amount of substance left after one hour
$=\dfrac{\mathrm{A}_{0}}{(2)^{\mathrm{n}}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{A}_{0}}{(2)^{4}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{A}_{0}}{16}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The reaction of ozone with oxygen atoms in the presence of chlorine atoms can occur by a two step process shown below:
${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{(}}\,{\rm{g)  +  C}}{{\rm{l}}^ \bullet }{\rm{(g)}} \to {{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(}}\,{\rm{g)  +  Cl}}{{\rm{O}}^ \bullet }{\rm{(g)}}{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{i}}}$
${\text{ = 5}}.{\text{2}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{\text{9}}}{\text{Lmo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}\,{{\text{s}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}{\text{Cl}}{{\text{O}}^ \bullet }({\text{g}}){\text{ + }}{{\text{O}}^ \bullet }({\text{g}}) \to $
${{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}(\,{\text{g}}){\text{ + C}}{{\text{l}}^ \bullet }({\text{g}}){{\text{k}}_{\text{i}}}$
${\rm{  =   2}}{\rm{.6 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{10}}}}{\rm{Lmo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{   -   1}}}}\,{{\rm{s}}^{{\rm{   -   1}}}}$
The closest rate constant for the overall reaction
$\mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}^{\bullet}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is:
explanation: The rate constant of overall reaction depends on slowest step. Hence equation(1) is slowest step. Option(2) is correct.
subject: Chemistry

question:
For a first order reaction, the time required for completion of 90% reaction is '${\rm{x}}$' times the half life of the reaction. The value of '${\rm{x}}$' is
(Given: $\ln 10=2.303$ and $\log 2=0.3010$ )
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{t}_{90 \%}=\mathrm{xt}_{1 / 2}$
$\dfrac{t_{90 \%}}{t_{1 / 2}}=x$
$\dfrac{\dfrac{2.303}{k} \cdot \log \dfrac{100}{10}}{\dfrac{0.693}{k}}=x \Rightarrow x=3.32$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The rate of a first order reaction depends on the concentration of the reactant raised to the first power.
Decomposition of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ follows zero order reaction.
explanation: Decomposition of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ follows $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction.

Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
If in a zero order reaction, the concentration of the reactant is doubled. The half-life period is also doubled.
For a zero order reaction, the rate of reaction is independent of initial concentration.
explanation: For a zero order reaction, $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]}{2 \mathrm{k}}$.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:

Reason: The plot of log of its partial pressure versus time is linear with slope, ${\rm{ - }}\frac{{\rm{k}}}{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.303}}}}$ and having intercept equal to $\log \mathrm{P}$.

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: The rate constant of a pseudounimolecular reaction has the units of the rate constant of a second order reaction.

Reason: A pseudounimolecular reaction is a reaction second order in which one of the reactant is present in large excess..
explanation:
Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
$\begin{gathered}\left(k^{\prime}=k\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]\right) \text { so that } k^{\prime}=k /\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]=\text { time }^{-1} / \mathrm{mol} \\L^{-1}=\mathrm{Lmol}^{-1} \text { time }^{-1}\end{gathered}$

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: Half-life of a certain radioactive element is 100 days. After 200 days, dfraction left undecayed will be $25 \%$.

Reason: $\dfrac{C_{1}}{C_{0}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n}$, where symbols have standard meaning.
explanation:
Numbering of half-lives
$\begin{gathered}=n=\dfrac{t}{T}=\dfrac{200}{100}=2 \\\dfrac{N}{N_{0}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n} \\\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{4}=\dfrac{1}{4} \times 100 \%\end{gathered}$

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.

subject: Chemistry

question: The rate of a first-order reaction is 0.04 $\mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at 10 seconds and $0.03 \mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at 20 seconds after initiation of the reaction. The half-life period of the reaction is:
explanation:
$k=\dfrac{2.303}{\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)} \log \dfrac{\left(a-x_{1}\right)}{\left(a-x_{2}\right)}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{2.303}{(20-10)} \log \left(\dfrac{0.04}{0.03}\right) \\& =\dfrac{2.303 \times 0.1249}{10} \\& \dfrac{2.303 \times \log 2}{t_{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{2.303 \times 0.1249}{10} \\& t_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.3010 \times 10}{0.1249}=24.1 \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The correct difference between first- and second-order reactions is that
explanation:
$t_{1 / 2} \alpha \dfrac{1}{[A]_{0}^{n-1}}$
For $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction $n=1$ $\therefore t_{1 / 2} \alpha \dfrac{1}{[A]_{0}^{1-1}}$
$\therefore t_{1 / 2}$ is independent of $[A]_{0}$
for 2 nd order reaction $n=2$
$\therefore t_{1 / 2} \alpha \dfrac{1}{[A]_{0}^{2-1}}$
$\therefore t_{1 / 2} \alpha \dfrac{1}{[A]_{0}^{1}}$

options.text: A first-order reaction can be catalyzed; a second-order reaction cannot be catalyzed
The rate of a first-order reaction does depend on reactant concentrations; the rate of a second-order reaction does not depend on reactantconcentrations
The half-life of a first-order reaction does not depend on $[A]_{0}$; the half-life of a second-order reaction does depend on $[A]_{0}$
The rate of a first-order reaction does not depend on reactant concentrations; the rate of a second-order reaction does depend on reactant concentrations

subject: Chemistry

question: When initial concentration of the reactant is doubled, the half-life period of a zero order reaction
explanation: According to zero order reaction, half life time is directly proportional to the initial concentration of the reactant.
subject: Chemistry

question: If the rate constant for a first order reaction is $\mathrm{k}$, the time $(\mathrm{t})$ required for the completion of $99 \%$ of the reaction is given by:
explanation:
First order rate constant is given as,
$k=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log \dfrac{\left[A_{0}\right]}{[A]_{t}}$
$99 \%$ completed reaction,
$k=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log \dfrac{100}{1}$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \log 10^{2}$
$k=\dfrac{2.303}{t} \times 2 \log 10$
$t=\dfrac{2.303}{k} \times 2 \dfrac{4.606}{k}$
$t=\dfrac{4.606}{k}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate constant for a first order reaction is $4.606 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The time required to reduce $2.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of the reactant to $0.2 \mathrm{~g}$ is:
subject: Chemistry

question: For a first order reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ Products, initial concentration of $\mathrm{A}$ is $0.1 \mathrm{M}$, which becomes $0.001 \mathrm{M}$ after 5 minutes. Rate constant for the reaction in $\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ Products
Initial conc. $[\mathrm{A}]_{0}=0.1 \mathrm{M}$
Conc. After $5 \min [\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=0.001 \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{t}=5 \mathrm{~min}$.
For first order reaction
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \left(\dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{o}}}{[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}\right)$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{5} \log \left(\dfrac{0.1}{0.001}\right)$
$\mathrm{k}=0.9212 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The integrated rate equation for first order reaction, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ product, is
explanation:
The integrated rate equation for first order reaction, $\mathrm{A} \longrightarrow$ product is $\mathrm{k}=-\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \operatorname{In} \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}$.
For 1 st order reactions, $\mathrm{R} \longrightarrow \mathrm{P}$
Rate $=\dfrac{-\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{R}]$
On integrating this equation (1) we get
$\operatorname{In}[\mathrm{R}]=-\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{I}$
At $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{R}=[\mathrm{R}]_{0,} \operatorname{In}[\mathrm{R}]_{0}=-\mathrm{k} \times 0+\mathrm{I}$
$\operatorname{In}[\mathrm{R}]_{0}=\mathrm{I}$
Substituting the value of I in (1) and on rearrangement, we get
$\operatorname{In}[\mathrm{R}]=-\mathrm{kt}+\operatorname{InR}_{0}$
and, $\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \operatorname{In} \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{R}_{0}\right]}{[\mathrm{R}]}$
- Rate of Disappearance
subject: Chemistry

question: A first order reaction is $25 \%$ completed in 40 minutes. What is the rate constant $\mathrm{k}$ for the reaction?
explanation:
Given,
$\operatorname{Time}(\mathrm{t})=40 \mathrm{~min}$
$a=100$
$(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})=100-25=75$
First order reaction,
$\operatorname{Rate}(\mathrm{k})=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \left(\dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}\right)=\dfrac{2.303 \times \log 1.33}{40}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A first order reaction has a rate constant $0.00813 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$. How long will it take for $60 \%$ completion?
explanation:
Given,
Rate constant $(\mathrm{k})=0.00813 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
According to first order reaction, the relation is
$\mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}$
As, $\mathrm{a}=100,(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})=100-60=40$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{0.00813} \log \dfrac{100}{40}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{2.303}{0.00813} \times \log 2.5[\log 2.5=0.3979] \\& =112.7 \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Time required for 100 percent completion of a zero order reaction is
explanation:
The concentration of reactant decreases linearly with time.
$[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=[\mathrm{A}]_{0}-\mathrm{kt}$
The time required for the zero order reaction to be complete i.e., time at which $[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}$ is zero i.e.,
${{\rm{t}}_{{\rm{completion}}}}{\rm{ = }}\left[ {{{\rm{A}}_{\rm{0}}}} \right]{\rm{k}}\,\,{\rm{or}}\,\,{\rm{a/k}}\,\,{\rm{if[A}}{{\rm{]}}_{\rm{0}}}{\rm{ = a}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For first order reaction the concentration of reactant decreases from 0.2 to $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ in 100 minutes. What is the rate constant of the reaction?
explanation:
Given,
$\operatorname{Time}(\mathrm{t})=100 \mathrm{~min}$
$\begin{aligned}& {\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]=0.2 \mathrm{M}} \\& {[\mathrm{A}]=0.1 \mathrm{M}}\end{aligned}$
According first order reaction,
rate constant $(\mathrm{k})=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]}{[\mathrm{A}]}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{2.303}{100 \min } \log \dfrac{[0.2]}{[0.1]} \\& =\dfrac{2.303}{100 \mathrm{~min}} \times 0.3010[\because \log 2=0.3010] \\& =6.93 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The relationship between rate constant and half-life period of zero order reaction is given by
explanation:
The relationship between rate constant (k) and half-life period of zero order reaction is written as,
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]}{2 \mathrm{k}}$
where, $\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]$ is the initial concentration and $\mathrm{k}=$ rate constant for zero order reaction.
subject: Chemistry

question: Half-life period of a first-order reaction is 1386 seconds. The specific rate constant of the reaction is
subject: Chemistry

question: If $50 \%$ of the reactant is converted into a product in a first order reaction in 25 minutes, how much of it would react in 100 minutes?
explanation:
Half-life is 25 mins.
$100 \stackrel{25 \mathrm{~min}}{\longrightarrow} 50 \stackrel{25 \mathrm{~min}}{\longrightarrow} 25 \stackrel{25 \mathrm{~min}}{\longrightarrow} 12.5$
$\stackrel{25 \min }{\longrightarrow} 6.25$
$6.25 \%$ remaining i.e., $93.75 \%$ reacted.
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate constant and half-life of a first order reaction are related to each other as
explanation:
For first order reaction,
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]}{[\mathrm{A}]}$
At $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2,}[\mathrm{~A}]=\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right] / 2$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}} \log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right] / 2} ; \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}} \log 2$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}} \text { or } \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: For the reaction $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$, the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ is $6.25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The rate of formation of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ will be respectively.
subject: Chemistry

question: The concentration of a reactant X decreases from $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ to $0.005 \mathrm{M}$ in 40 minutes. If the reaction follows $1^{\text {st }}$ order kinetics, the rate of the reaction when the concentration of $\mathrm{X}$ is 0.01 $\mathrm{M}$ will be
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the value of rate constant of first order reaction, if it takes 15 minutes for consumption of $20 \%$ of reactants?
explanation:
Given, $t=15 \mathrm{~min}$ for $20 \%$ of reactant to react. For first order reaction,
Rate constant $(\mathrm{k})=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{\left[\mathrm{R}_{0}\right]}{[\mathrm{R}]}$
where,
$\left[\mathrm{R}_{0}\right]=$ original amount of reactant
$[R]=$ reactant remaining unreacted
So, $\left[\mathrm{R}_{0}\right]=100$
$[R]=80(20 \%$ reacted $)$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{15 \min } \log \left[\dfrac{100}{80}\right]$
$=1.48 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A first order reaction is $20 \%$ complete in 10 minutes. The rate constant of the reaction is
explanation:
$k=\left(\dfrac{2.303}{t}\right) \log \left(\dfrac{a}{a-x}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}& =\left(\dfrac{2.303}{10}\right) \log \left(\dfrac{100}{80}\right) \\& =0.0223 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
$=40 \mathrm{~min}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The time required for $60 \%$ completion of a first order reaction is $50 \mathrm{~min}$. The time required for $93.6 \%$ completion of the same reaction will be
explanation:
For first order reaction,
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}$
where, $a=$ initial concentration of the reactant, $\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}=$ concentration of the reactant after time, t.
For $60 \%$ completion of reaction,
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{50} \log \dfrac{100}{40} \\& \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{50} \times 0.397=0.0183\end{aligned}$
For $93.6 \%$ completion of reaction,
$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{k} & =\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{100}{6.4} \\0.0183 & =\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{100}{6.4} \\\mathrm{t} & =\dfrac{2.303}{0.0183} \times 1.193 \\\mathrm{t} & =150 \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If the rate constant for a first order reaction is $\mathrm{k}$, the time $(\mathrm{t})$ required for the completion of $99 \%$ of the reaction is givne by
explanation:
$\mathrm{t}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}} \log \dfrac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{R}]}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}} \log \left(\dfrac{100}{1}\right) \\& =\dfrac{4.606}{\mathrm{k}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If $60 \%$ of a first-order reaction was completed in $60 \mathrm{~min}, 50 \%$ of the same reaction would be completed in approximately
explanation:
$60=\dfrac{2.303}{k} \log \dfrac{100}{40}$
$\begin{aligned}t_{1 / 2} & =\dfrac{2.303}{k} \log \dfrac{100}{50} \\\therefore \dfrac{60}{t} & =\dfrac{0.40}{0.310} \\\therefore \mathrm{t} & =45 \text { minute }\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In a first order reaction, the concentration of the reactant is reduced to $12.5 \%$ in one hour. When was it half completed?
explanation:
For a first order reaction,
$\begin{aligned}k & =\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]}=\dfrac{2.303}{60} \log \dfrac{100}{12.5} \\& =0.0346 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
Now $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}=\dfrac{0.693}{0.0346}=20 \mathrm{~min}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The following data is obtained during the first order thermal decompsoition of
$2 \mathrm{~A}_{(\mathrm{g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}$
at constant volume and temperature.
The rate constant in ${\rm{mi}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ is
explanation:
$\mathop {{\rm{2}}{{\rm{A}}_{\left( {\rm{g}} \right)}}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{2(l - x)}}}\\{{\rm{(2 - 2x)}}}\end{array}} \mathop {{{\rm{B}}_{{\rm{(g)}}}}}\limits_{\rm{x}} {\rm{ + }}{{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{(s)}}}}$
$2(1-x)$$(2-2 x)$
At the end of reaction, only 1 mole of gas is present whose pressure is 200 pascal.
$=2-2 x+x=2-x$
But the pressure of 2 moles $=400$ pascal
$\therefore 400-x=300 \Rightarrow x=100$
$\therefore$ Pressure due to $2-2 \mathrm{x}$ moles of $\mathrm{A}$
$=400-200=200$ pascal
$\therefore \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \left[\dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}\right]=\dfrac{2.303}{10} \log \left[\dfrac{400}{200}\right]$
$=\dfrac{2.303}{10} \log 2=\dfrac{0.693}{10}=0.0693 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{P}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{Q}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{R}_{(\mathrm{g})}$, follows first order kinetics with a half-life of $69.3 \mathrm{~s}$ at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Starting from the gas ' $A$ ' enclosed in a container at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and at a pressure of $0.4 \mathrm{~atm}$, the total pressure of the system after $230 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
explanation:
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=69.3 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{t}=230 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{a}=0.4 \mathrm{~atm}$
For first order reaction,
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{0.693}{69.3}=0.01 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}=\dfrac{2.303}{230} \log \dfrac{0.4}{0.4-\mathrm{x}} \\& 0.01=\dfrac{2.303}{230} \log \dfrac{0.4}{0.4-\mathrm{x}}=0.01 \log \dfrac{0.4}{0.4-\mathrm{x}} \\& \dfrac{0.01}{0.01}=\log \dfrac{0.4}{0.4-\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow 1=\log \dfrac{0.4}{0.4-\mathrm{x}}\end{aligned}$
Taking Antilog of 1 , we get
$\dfrac{0.4}{0.4-x} \Rightarrow 10=\dfrac{0.4}{0.4-x} \Rightarrow x=0.36$
For the given reaction,
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\text{Initial}}}&{{\text{0}}{\text{.4}}}&{\text{0}}&{\text{0}}&{\text{0}} \\ {{\text{Final}}}&{{\text{0}}{\text{.4 - 0}}{\text{.36}}}&{{\text{0}}{\text{.36}}}&{{\text{0}}{\text{.36}}}&{{\text{0}}{\text{.36}}} \end{array}$
Total pressure $=(0.4-0.36)+(3 \times 0.36)=1.12 \mathrm{~atm}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate equation for a reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ is ${\rm{r = k[A}}{{\rm{]}}^{\rm{0}}}$. If the initial concentration of the reactant is a mol $\mathrm{dm}^{-3}$, the half life period of the reaction is
explanation:
According to the rate equation given, the reaction is of zero order.
For a zero order reaction, $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \propto \mathrm{a}$ or $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{k}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Half-life period of a first order reaction, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ product is $6.93 \mathrm{~h}$. What is the value of rate constant?
explanation:
We have the formula for half-life of a first order reaction.
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}$
where, $\mathrm{k}=$ rate constant
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \quad 6.93 \mathrm{~h} & =\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{0.693}{6.93 \mathrm{~h}} \\\mathrm{k} & =0.1 \mathrm{~h}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In a first-order reaction ${\rm{A}} \to {\rm{B}}$, if $\mathrm{k}$ is the rate constant and initial concentration of the reactant is ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.5M}}$, then half-life is
explanation:
For Ist order reaction,
$\begin{aligned}& k=\dfrac{2.303 \log _{10} 2}{t_{1 / 2}} \\& t_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{k}=\dfrac{\ln 2}{k}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Half-life period of a first order reaction is 10 min. Starting with initial concentration $12 \mathrm{M}$, the rate after $20 \mathrm{~min}$ is
explanation:
$\mathop {{\text{12M}}}\limits_{{\text{ Initial}}\,\,{\text{conc }}} \xrightarrow{{{{\text{t}}_{{\text{//2}}}}}}{\text{6}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{t}}_{{\text{/2}}}}}}{\text{3M}}$
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=10 \min$
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{0.693}{10}=0.0693 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
As $t_{1 / 2}$ is $10 \mathrm{~min}$, after 20 minutes the concentration will be $3 \mathrm{M}$.
Hence, Rate $=0.0693 \times 3 \mathrm{Mmin}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The half-life period of a first order reaction is 60 minutes. What percentage will be left over after 240 minutes?
explanation:
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}=\mathrm{k} \Rightarrow \dfrac{0.693}{60}=\mathrm{k}$
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{k}=0.01155 \mathrm{~min}^{-1} \\& \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log \left(\dfrac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}\right)\end{aligned}$
Let the initial amount (a) be 100 .
$\begin{gathered}0.01155 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}=\dfrac{2.303}{240 \min } \log \left(\dfrac{100}{a-x}\right) \\\dfrac{0.01155 \min ^{-1} \times 40 \min }{2.303}=\log \left(\dfrac{100}{a-x}\right) \\1.204=\log 100-\log (a-x) \\1.204=2-\log (a-x) \\\log (a-x)=2-1.204 \\\log (a-x)=0.796\end{gathered}$
or $\quad(a-x)=6.25 \%$
subject: Chemistry

question: The rate constant for a first order reaction is $7.0 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. If initial concentration of reactant is $0.080 \mathrm{M}$, what is the half life of reaction?
explanation:
Given,
$\mathrm{K}=7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}^{-1},[\mathrm{~A}]_{0}=0.08 \mathrm{M}$
Half life $\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)$ of a first order reaction is independent of initial concentation and is given as
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}=\dfrac{0.693}{7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}}=990 \mathrm{~s}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Half-life of a reaction is found to be inversely    proportional to the fifth power is initial concentration,  the order of reaction is
explanation: $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \propto \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-1}} ; \mathrm{n}=6$

explanation:
Plot of ${{\text{t}}_{{\text{1/2}}}}\,\,{\text{vs}}\,\,{{\text{[R]}}_{\text{0}}}$ for a reaction is a straight line parallel to x-axis, means ${{\text{t}}_{1/2}}$ vs ${[{\text{R}}]_0}$ is independent of ${[{\text{R}}]_0}$ hence, the reaction is first order.

Reason: The slope of the line in a plot of $\ln [\mathrm{R}]$ versus time for a first-order reaction is equal to the negative of the rate constant .

question: Half-life periods for a reaction at initial concentrations of 0.1 M and 0.01 M are 5 and 50 minutes, respectively. The order of reaction is
explanation:
Given, ${\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)_1} = 5$ minutes, ${\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)_2} = 50$ minutes
$\mathrm{a}_{1}=0.1 \mathrm{M}, \mathrm{a}_{2}=0.01 \mathrm{M}$
$\frac{{{{\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)}_1}}}{{{{\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)}_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{{\rm{a}}_2}}}{{{{\rm{a}}_1}}}} \right)^{{\rm{n}} - 1}} \Rightarrow \frac{5}{{50}} = {\left( {\frac{{0.01}}{{0.1}}} \right)^{{\rm{n}} - 1}}$
$(10)^{1}=(10)^{\mathrm{n}-1}$ or $1=\mathrm{n}-1$ or $\mathrm{n}=2$
$\therefore$ It is second order reaction.

question: Following is the graph between $\log \,\,{{\rm{t}}_{{\rm{1/2}}}}$ and log a (a initial concentration) for a given reaction at ${\mathrm{27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$. Find the order of reaction.
explanation:
${\mathrm{t_{1 / 2} \propto \dfrac{1}{a_{0}^{n-1}}}}$
${\mathrm{t_{1 / 2}=k \cdot \dfrac{1}{a_{0}^{n-1}}}}$
${\mathrm{\log t_{1 / 2}=\log k-(n-1) \log a_{0}}}$ $[y=c+m x]$
${\mathrm{\tan 45^{\circ}=-(n-1)=-(0-1)}}$
${\mathrm{\tan 45^{\circ}=1}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore n=0}}$

question: For the reaction ${\mathrm{A+2 B \longrightarrow C}}$, the reaction rate is doubled if the concentration of A is doubled. The rate is increased by four times when concentrations of both A and B are increased by four times. The order of the reaction is ____.
explanation:
Rate of the given reaction is doubled when concentration of ' A ' is doubled and it is quadrupled when concentrations of ' A ' and ' B ' are raised four times.
Let the rate, ${\rm{R = k}}{[{\rm{A}}]^{\rm{x}}}{[{\rm{B}}]^{\rm{y}}}\,\,\,\,(1)$
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ When concentration of ${\mathrm{A}}$ is doubled, the new rate ${\mathrm{\left(R_{1}\right)}}$ will be
${{\rm{R}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = k}}{[{\rm{2A}}]^{\rm{x}}}{[{\rm{B}}]^{\rm{y}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
But ${\mathrm{\mathrm{R}_{1}=2 \mathrm{R}}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\rm{k}}{[{\rm{2A}}]^{\rm{x}}}{[{\rm{B}}]^{\rm{y}}}{\rm{ = 2k}}{[{\rm{A}}]^{\rm{x}}}{[{\rm{B}}]^{\rm{y}}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} [{\rm{From(1)}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and }}({\rm{2}})]$
${\mathrm{\therefore k 2^{\mathrm{x}}[A]^{\mathrm{x}}[B]^{\mathrm{y}}=2 \mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{x}}[B]^{\mathrm{y}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 2^{x}=2}}$ and thus ${\mathrm{x=1}}$
Similarly, when concentrations of both ${\mathrm{A}}$ and ${\mathrm{B}}$ are quadrupled, the new rate ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{R}_{2}\right)}}$ will be${{\rm{R}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = k}}{[{\rm{4}}\,{\rm{A}}]^{\rm{x}}}{[{\rm{4}}\,{\rm{B}}]^{\rm{y}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
But ${\mathrm{\mathrm{R}_{2}=4 \mathrm{R}}}$
$\therefore \,\,{\rm{k}}{[{\rm{4}}\,{\rm{A}}]^{\rm{x}}}{[{\rm{4}}\,{\rm{B}}]^{\rm{y}}}{\rm{ = 4k}}{[{\rm{A}}]^{\rm{x}}}{[{\rm{B}}]^{\rm{y}}}\quad $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[From(1)\,and\,(3)]$
${\mathrm{K 4^{x}[A]^{x} 4^{y}[B]^{y}=4 k[A]^{x}[B]^{y}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore 4^{x} \cdot 4^{y}=4}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 4.4^{\mathrm{y}}=4 \quad(\because \mathrm{x}=1)}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 4^{y}=1}}$ and thus ${\mathrm{y=0}}$
Substituting the values of ${\mathrm{x}}$ and ${\mathrm{y}}$ in (1), the rate expression for the given reaction is
rate, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{1}[\mathrm{~B}]^{0} \quad \therefore \quad \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]}}$
Thus, the overall order of the given reaction is 1 .
subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods to Determine Order of Reaction
subtopic: Methods to Determine Order of Reaction
parent_topic: Methods to Determine Order of Reaction

question: The half-lives of two samples are $0.1 \mathrm{~s}$ and 0.8 s and their respective concentrations are $400 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ and $50 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. The order of the reaction is
explanation:
We know that,
$\frac{{{{\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)}_1}}}{{{{\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)}_2}}} = {\left[ {\frac{{{{\rm{a}}_2}}}{{{{\rm{a}}_1}}}} \right]^{{\rm{n}} - 1}}$
where, ${\rm{n}} = $ order of the reaction
Given, ${\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)_1} = 0.1\,{\rm{s}},{{\rm{a}}_1} = 400\,{\rm{mol\,}}{{\rm{L}}^{ - 1}}$
${\left( {{{\rm{t}}_{1/2}}} \right)_2} = 0.8\,{\rm{s}},{{\rm{a}}_2} = 50\,{\rm{mol\,}}{{\rm{L}}^{ - 1}}$
On substituting the values,
$\dfrac{0.1}{0.8}=\left(\dfrac{50}{400}\right)^{n-1}$
On taking log both sides
${\mathop{\rm l}\nolimits} {\rm{og}}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.1}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.8}}}}{\rm{ = (n - 1)log}}\frac{{{\rm{50}}}}{{{\rm{400}}}} \Rightarrow {\rm{log}}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{8}}}$
${\rm{ = (n - 1)log}}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{8}}}{\rm{ - 0}}{\rm{.90 = (n - 1)( - 0}}{\rm{.90)}}$
${\rm{n - 1 = 1}} \Rightarrow {\rm{n = 2}}$
subject: Chemistry

subtopic: Methods to Determine Order of Reaction
parent_topic: Methods to Determine Order of Reaction

question:
A student has studied the decomposition of a gas $\mathrm{AB}_{3}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. He obtained the following data.

The order of the reaction is
explanation:
${{\text{t}}_{1/2}} \propto {\left( {{{\text{C}}_0}} \right)^{1 - {\text{n}}}}$
In terms of pressure,
$\dfrac{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{1}}{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{1}}{\mathrm{p}_{2}}\right)^{1-\mathrm{n}}$
$\dfrac{4}{2}=\left(\dfrac{50}{100}\right)^{1-n}$
or $(2)^{1}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{1-n}$
or $(2)^{1}=(2)^{\mathrm{n}-1}$
On equating, we get
$1=\mathrm{n}-1$
$\mathrm{n}=2$

question: For reaction, $\mathrm{aA} \rightarrow \mathrm{xp}$, when $[\mathrm{A}]=2.2 \mathrm{mM}$ the rate was found to be $2.4 \mathrm{mMs}^{-1}$. On reducing concentration of $\mathrm{A}$  to half, the rate changes to $0.6 \mathrm{mMs}^{-1}$. The order of reaction with respect to $\mathrm{A}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{aA} \rightarrow \mathrm{xP}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { Rate } \propto[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{a}} \\& \text { Initially }[\mathrm{A}]_{1}=2.2 \mathrm{mM}, \\& \mathrm{r}_{1}=2.4 \mathrm{mM} / \mathrm{s} \\& \text { If }[\mathrm{A}]_{2}=\dfrac{2.2}{2}=1.1 \mathrm{mM}, \\& \mathrm{r}_{2}=0.6 \mathrm{mM} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
We have, $\dfrac{\mathrm{r}_{1}}{\mathrm{r}_{2}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{1}^{\mathrm{a}}}{[\mathrm{A}]_{2}^{\mathrm{a}}}(\mathrm{a}$ is the order $)$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{2.4}{0.6}=\dfrac{(2.2)^{\mathrm{a}}}{(1.1)^{\mathrm{a}}} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{2.4}{0.6}=(2)^{\mathrm{a}}\end{aligned}$
or $4=2^{\mathrm{a}}$
$\Rightarrow 2^{2}=2^{\mathrm{a}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=2$
$\therefore$ Order is 2 .

question:
For the non-stoichiometric reaction, ${\rm{2A + B}} \to {\rm{C + D}}$, the following kinetic data were obtained in three separate experiments, all at $298 \mathrm{~K}$.
The rate law for the formation of C is :
explanation:
Rate law equation is given by
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d c}{d t}=k[A]^{x}[B]^{y} \\& \therefore \quad \dfrac{d c}{d t} \text { for } \mathrm{C} \text { at various concentration are } \\& 1.2 \times 10^{-3}=k(0.1)^{x}(0.1)^{y} \\& 1.2 \times 10^{-3}=k(0.1)^{x}(0.2)^{y} \\& 2.4 \times 10^{-3}=k(0.2)^{x}(0.1)^{y}\end{aligned}$
Solving for $\mathrm{x}$ [divide Eq. (iii) by Eq. (i)]
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}=\dfrac{k(0.2)^{x}(0.1)^{y}}{k(0.1)^{x}(0.1)^{y}} \\& 2=(2) x \\\Rightarrow & x=1\end{aligned}$
Solving by y [divide Eq. (3) by Eq. (1)]
$\begin{gathered}\dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}=\dfrac{k(0.2)^{x}(0.1)^{y}}{k(0.1)^{x}(0.1)^{y}} \\2=(2)^{x}\left(\dfrac{0.1}{0.2}\right)^{y} \\1=(0.5)^{y} \\\Rightarrow \quad(1)^{0}=(0.5)^{y} \\y=0 \\\text { Thus } \dfrac{d c}{d t}=k[A]^{1}[B]^{0} \\\dfrac{d c}{d t}=k[A]^{1}\end{gathered}$
subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods to Determine Order of Reaction
subtopic: Methods to Determine Order of Reaction
parent_topic: Methods to Determine Order of Reaction

question: Consider a reaction ${\text{aG + bH}} \to $ products. When concentration of both the reactants $G$ and $\mathrm{H}$ is doubled, the rate increases by eight times. Overall order $=$ Sum of orders wrt each reactant.
Let the order be $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}$ for $\mathrm{G}$ and $\mathrm{H}$ respectively. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954059/preptracker/questions/q_23187_explanation_1764954059288.png From expt. no., (1) and (3), the rate is doubled when conc. of $\mathrm{G}$ is doubled keeping that of $\mathrm{H}$ is constant i.e.,
$\text { rate } \propto[G] \quad \therefore x=1$
From expt. no., (2) and (3), $y=2$
$\therefore$ Overall order is 3 .

explanation:
$2 A+B \rightarrow$ Product
When concentration of $B$ is doubled, the halflife did not change, hence, reaction is of firstorder w.r.t. B. When concentration of $A$ is doubled, reaction rate is doubled, hence, reaction is of first-order w.r.t. A. Hence, overall order of reaction is $1+1=2$
So, unit of rate constant is $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ litre $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$.

$\frac{{{\text{0}}{\text{.10}}}}{{{\text{0}}{\text{.40}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{{\text{[0}}{\text{.30]}}}^{\text{y}}}}}{{{{{\text{[0}}{\text{.60]}}}^{\text{y}}}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{4}}}{\text{ = }}{\left[ {\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}} \right]^{\text{y}}}$

${\text{y = 2}}$

question:
The following results were obtained during kinetic studies of the reaction; $2 A+B \rightarrow$ products
The time (in minutes) required to consume half of $A$ is
explanation:
Assume ${\text{r = k[A}}{{\text{]}}^{\text{x}}}{{\text{[B]}}^{\text{y}}}$
Taking exp. I and II, we get order of [B]
$\begin{aligned}& \left(\dfrac{0.20}{0.25}\right)^{\mathrm{y}}=\dfrac{6.93 \times 10^{-3}}{6.93 \times 10^{-3}}=1 \\& \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{y}}=\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\right]^{0} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=0\end{aligned}$
Taking expanision I and III,
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{6.93 \times 10^{-3}}{1.386 \times 10^{-2}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{x} \\& x=1\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Overall order $=0+1=1$
Now, rate $=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{1}[\mathrm{~B}]^{0}$
$\begin{aligned}& 6.93 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{k}[0.10] \\& \mathrm{k}=\dfrac{6.93 \times 10^{-3}}{0.10}=0.0693 \mathrm{~min} \\& \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}=\dfrac{0.693}{0.0693}=10 \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$
To consume half of $A$ it becomes $\dfrac{10}{2}=5 \mathrm{~min}$

question: If concentration of reactant 'A' is increased by 10 times the rate of reaction becomes 100 times. What is the order of reaction, if rate law is, rate $=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{x}}$ ?
explanation:
Given,
$\text { Rate }_{\text {old }}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{x}}$
If concentration of reactant ' $\mathrm{A}$ ' is increased by 10 times that means,
Rate $_{\text {new }}=\mathrm{k}[10 \mathrm{~A}]^{\mathrm{x}}$
Rate of reaction becomes 100 times,
$100 \times$ rate $_{\text {old }}=\mathrm{k}[10 \mathrm{~A}]^{\mathrm{x}}$
From Eq. (1) and (2),
$\begin{aligned}100 \times \mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{x}} & =\mathrm{k}[10 \mathrm{~A}]^{\mathrm{x}} \\100 & =10^{\mathrm{x}} \\10^{2} & =10^{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2\end{aligned}$

question: For a chemical reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$, the rate of the reaction is $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, when the initial concentration is 0.05 ${\rm{mol}}\,\,{\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}\,{{\rm{s}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$. The rate of the same reaction is ${\rm{1}}{\rm{.6 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 2}}}}\,\,{\rm{mol}}\,{\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}\,{{\rm{s}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ when the initial concentration is ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.1}}\,{\rm{mol}}\,{\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{{\rm{ - 3}}}}{\rm{.}}$ The order of the reaction is
explanation:
Let the rate equation for the reaction be, rate $=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{n}}$
Where, $\mathrm{k}=$ rate constant, $\mathrm{n}=$ order of reaction $[\mathrm{A}]=$ concentration of reactant
given, rate $_{1}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
$\begin{aligned}& {\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]=0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}} \\& \text { rate }_{11}=1.6 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\& {\left[A_{0}\right]=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}} \\& \therefore 2 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{k}[0.05]^{\mathrm{n}} \\& 1.6 \times 10^{-2}=\mathrm{k}[0.1]^{\mathrm{n}}\end{aligned}$
Divide (1) by (2)
$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-2}}=\dfrac{[0.05]^{\mathrm{n}}}{[0.1]^{\mathrm{n}}}=\dfrac{[0.05]^{\mathrm{n}}}{2^{\mathrm{n}}[0.05]^{\mathrm{n}}} \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2^{\mathrm{n}}}=\dfrac{1}{8} \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=3\end{gathered}$
$\therefore$ Order of reaction $=3$

question:
For a reaction, $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow$ products, the rate of the reaction at various concentrations are given
The rate law for the above reaction is
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow$ Products
Rate $=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\alpha}[\mathrm{B}]^{\beta}$
From expt. No. 1
${\rm{2 = k}}{[{\rm{0}}.{\rm{2}}]^\alpha }{[{\rm{0}}.{\rm{2}}]^\beta }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,({\rm{1}})$
From expt. No. 2
${\rm{4 = k}}{[{\rm{0}}.{\rm{2}}]^\alpha }{[{\rm{0}}.{\rm{4}}]^\beta }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,({\rm{2}})$
Dividing eq. (1) and (2) we get,
$\dfrac{2}{4}=\dfrac{\mathrm{k}[0.2]^{\alpha}[0.2]^{\beta}}{\mathrm{k}[0.2]^{\alpha}[0.4]^{\beta}} \text { or } \dfrac{1}{2}=\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\right]^{\beta} \Rightarrow \beta=1$
From expt. No. 3
${\rm{36 = k}}{[{\rm{0}}{\rm{.6}}]^\alpha }{[{\rm{0}}.{\rm{4}}]^\beta }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(3)}}$
dividing eq. (2) and (3) we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{4}{36}=\dfrac{\mathrm{k}[0.2]^{\alpha}[0.4]^{\beta}}{\mathrm{k}[0.6]^{\alpha}[0.4]^{\beta}} \text { or } \\& \dfrac{1}{9}=\left[\dfrac{1}{3}\right]^{\alpha} \Rightarrow \alpha=2\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow$ Rate law for the given reaction is
$\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{2}[\mathrm{~B}]^{1}$

question: For a reaction $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$ if the concentration of A is doubled without altering the concentration of $B$, the rate gets doubled. If the concentration of $\mathrm{B}$ is increased by nine times without altering the concentration of $\mathrm{A}$, the rate gets tripled. The order of the reaction is
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$
${\rm{r = k[A}}{{\rm{]}}^{\rm{\alpha }}}{{\rm{[B]}}^{\rm{\beta }}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(1)}}$
${\rm{2r = k[A}}{{\rm{]}}^{{\rm{2\alpha }}}}{{\rm{[B]}}^{\rm{\beta }}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(2)}}$
${\rm{3r = k[A}}{{\rm{]}}^{\rm{\alpha }}}{{\rm{[9\,B]}}^{\rm{\beta }}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(3)}}$
Dividing eqn. (2) by eqn. (1)
$2=2^{\alpha} \text { or, } \alpha=1$
Dividing eqn. (3) by eqn. (1)
$3=9^{\beta}$ or, $3=3^{2 \beta}$ or, $2 \beta=1$ or $\beta=1 / 2$
$\therefore$ Order of the reaction $=1+\dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{3}{2}$

question:
A possible mechanism for the gaseous reaction $2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NO} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{N}_{2}$ is
Step 1: $2 \mathrm{NO} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$
Step 2: $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (slow)
Step 3: $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
The rate law for this reaction is
explanation:
The rate of reaction, in a multistep reaction is determined by slowest step. So,
${\rm{R}} = {{\rm{k}}_1}\left[ {\,{{\rm{N}}_2}{{\rm{O}}_2}} \right]\left[ {{{\rm{H}}_2}} \right]{\rm{ }}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ is not the part of overall reaction. Hence, its concentration can be calculated from step 1 i.e., $\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right]=\mathrm{k}_{2}[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}$
[where, $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ is rate constant for step 1]
By substituting concentration of $\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right]$ in (1).
We get, $\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{k}_{1} \mathrm{k}_{2}[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]$ or
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{NO}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right] \quad\left(\right.$ where, $\left.\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{k}_{1} \times \mathrm{k}_{2}\right)$
subject: Chemistry

question: In the sequence of reaction,  $\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{1}} \mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{2}} \mathrm{Y} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{3}} \mathrm{Z}$    $\mathrm{k}_{3}>\mathrm{k}_{2}>\mathrm{k}_{1}$, then the rate determining step is
explanation:
Sequence of reaction,
$\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{1}} \mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{2}} \mathrm{Y} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{3}} \mathrm{Z}$
In chemical kinetics, the overall rate of a reaction is often approximately determined by slowest step, known as the rate determining step or rate limiting step. When 'k' is small, the step will be slowest.
So, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{X}$ is rate determining step.

question:
For a hypothetical reaction, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$
$\mathrm{A} \underset{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\stackrel{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} \mathrm{~B}$ (Fast) $\quad $ $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{3}} \mathrm{C}$ (Slow) Rate law for this reaction is
explanation:
Rate of reaction is directly proportional to the product of concentration of reactants. Rate law is always written for slow step in the reaction.
For $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{3}} \mathrm{C}$ (slow)
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{k}_{3}[\mathrm{~A}][\mathrm{B}] \quad$ but $[\mathrm{B}]=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]$
So, rate $=\mathrm{k}_{3}[\mathrm{~A}] \mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]$
$\therefore \quad$ rate $ = {{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}{[\,{\text{A}}]^2}$

question:
For the chemical reaction, $2 \mathrm{O}_{3} \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{O}_{2}$
The reaction proceed as follows:
${{\text{O}}_3} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{O}}_2} + {\text{O (fast)}}$
${\text{O}} + {{\text{O}}_3} \Rightarrow 2{{\text{O}}_2}{\text{ (slow)}}$
The order of reaction in rate law expression will be
explanation:
As slowest step is the rate determining step. Hence, from slow reaction,
${\text{r}} = {\text{k[O]}}\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
From fast reaction, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}} = \frac{{\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]{\text{[O]}}}}{{\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]}}$
$[{\rm{O]}} = \frac{{{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{C}}} \times \left[ {{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]}}{\rm{, i}}{\rm{.e}}{\rm{. }}$
${\text{r}} = \frac{{{\text{k}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}}\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}} \cdot \left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right] \Rightarrow {\text{k}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}} \cdot {\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]^{\text{2}}}{\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]^{\text{1}}}$
$\therefore \quad$ Rate ${\text{(r)}} = {{\text{k}}^\prime }{\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]^2} \cdot {\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]^{\text{1}}}$
$\left( {{{\text{k}}^\prime } = {{\text{K}}_{\text{C}}} \cdot {\text{k}}} \right)$
Hence, the order of reaction in rate law expression will be 1 .

question:
Read Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct option given below.

Assertion: As mole is the basic chemical unit, the concentration of the dissolved solute is usually specified in terms of number of moles of solute.

Reason: The total number of molecules of reactants involved in a balanced chemical equation is known as molecularity of the reaction.
explanation:
The number of moles of a solute present in one litre of solution is known is as molarity (M). The total no. of molecules of reactants present in a balanced chemical equation is known as molecularity. For example,
$\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \rightarrow \mathrm{PCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ (Unimolecular)
$2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2}$ (Biomolecular)
$\therefore$ Molarity and molecularity are used in different sense. There is no relation between assertion and reason.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: Molecularity has no meaning for a complex reaction.


Reason: The overall molecularity of a complex reaction is equal to the molecularity of the slowest step.
explanation: Molecularity, a concept applicable only to elementary reactions, signifies the number of reactant molecules colliding simultaneously in a single, straightforward step. A complex reactions, which involve multiple consecutive steps lacks a clear determination of molecularity due to the intricate nature of intermediate species and simultaneous encounters. So option (3) is correct.

question:
The reaction, $X_{2}+Y_{2} \rightarrow 2 X Y$ occurs by the following mechanism
(i) $X_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 X$ (fast)
(ii) $X+Y_{2} \rightarrow X Y+X$ (slow)
(iii) $X+Y \rightarrow X Y$ (fast)
What is the order of the reaction?
explanation:
From slowest step (rds),
${\rm{r = k}}\left[ {\rm{X}} \right]\left[ {{{\rm{Y}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]$
From fast step
${\text{K = }}\frac{{{{{\text{[X]}}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{\left[ {{{\text{X}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}}$
${\rm{[X] = }}{{\rm{K}}^{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}}}{\left[ {{{\rm{X}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]^{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}}}$
$\therefore \quad {\rm{r = k}}{{\rm{K}}^{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}}}{\left[ {{{\rm{X}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]^{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}}}\left[ {{{\rm{Y}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]$
${\rm{r = k'}}{\left[ {{{\rm{X}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]^{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}}}\left[ {{{\rm{Y}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]\left( {{\rm{k' = k}}{{\rm{K}}^{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}}}} \right)$
Order of the reaction ${\rm{ = 1 + 1/2 = 3/2}}\,\,{\rm{or}}\,\,{\rm{1}}{\rm{.5}}$

question:
Nitric oxide (NO) reacts with oxygen to produce nitrogen dioxide
${\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{(g)}}}}{\text{  +  }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}}\xrightarrow{{\text{K}}}{\text{2N}}{{\text{O}}_{{\text{2(g)}}}}$
If the mechanism of reaction is
${\text{NO  +  }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\mathop  \rightleftharpoons \limits_{\text{K}} {{\text{N}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{( fast )}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{  +  NO}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{1}}}}}$
${\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{  +  N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(slow)}}$
then rate law is
explanation:
From slowest step i.e., rds step,
Rate $=\mathrm{k}_{1}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right][\mathrm{NO}]$
$\mathrm{NO}_{3}$ is an intermediate, need to be replaced with $\mathrm{NO}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ by using fast step.
$K=\dfrac{\left[N O_{3}\right]}{[N O]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]} \Rightarrow\left[N O_{3}\right]=K[N O]\left[O_{2}\right]$
Substitute $\left[\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right]$ in eq. (1)
Rate $=k_{1} K[N O]\left[O_{2}\right][N O]$
Rate $=k^{\prime}[N O]^{2}\left[O_{2}\right] \quad\left[k^{\prime}=k_{1} K\right]$

question:
A chemical reaction proceeds into the following steps
Step I, $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{X}$ (fast)
Step II, ${\rm{X + B}} \to {\rm{Y(slow)}}$
Step III, $Y+B \rightleftharpoons$ Product (fast)
The rate law for the overall reaction is
explanation:
From slowest step i.e., rds step,
Rate $=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{X}][\mathrm{B}]$ $\mathrm{X}$ is an intermediate, need to be replaced with A by using fast step $I$.
$K=\dfrac{[X]}{[A]^{2}} \Rightarrow[X]=K[A]^{2}$
substitute $[X]$ in eq. (1)
Rate $=\mathrm{kK}[\mathrm{A}]^{2}[\mathrm{~B}]$
Rate $=\mathrm{k}^{1}[\mathrm{~A}]^{2}[\mathrm{~B}]$

question:

${\text{O + }}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}} \to {\text{2}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(slow)}}$
The rate law expression should be
explanation:
From Eq. (1), $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{O}]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]}$
or $[\mathrm{O}]=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}$
From Eq. (2), Rate law $=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right][\mathrm{O}]$
or $\mathrm{r}=\dfrac{\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$
or $\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k}^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{-1}\left[\mathrm{k}^{\prime} \mathrm{k}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}\right]$

question:
Consider the reaction
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(aq) + }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{\,S(aq)}} \to {{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{l}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{l}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + H}}{{\rm{S}}^{\rm{ - }}}$
The rate equation for this reaction is,
${\rm{ Rate   =  k}}\left[ {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]\left[ {{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{S}}} \right]$
Which of these mechanisms is/are consistent with this rate equation?
(I) ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}} \to {{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{l}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{l}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + H}}{{\rm{S}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ({\rm{slow}})$
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + H}}{{\rm{S}}^{\rm{ - }}} \to {{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{l}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\rm{ + S}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} ({\rm{fast}})$
${\text{(II)}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\,{\text{S}} \rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^{{\text{   +   }}}}{\text{   +   H}}{{\text{S}}^{{\text{   -   }}}}{\text{(fast}}\,\,{\text{equilibrium) }}$
${\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{  +  H}}{{\text{S}}^{{\text{  -  }}}} \to {\text{2C}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{  -  }}}}{\text{  +  }}{{\text{H}}^{{\text{  +  }}}}{\text{  +  S}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{(slow)}}$
explanation:
Slowest step is the rate-determining step. Thus, in case (I) rate law is given as rate $=k\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}\right]$
While for the reaction given in case (II), rate law is given as
$\text { rate }=k\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{-1} .$
Hence, only mechanism (I) is consistent with the given rate law.

A hypothetical reaction, ${{\rm{A}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{B}}_{\rm{2}}} \to {\rm{2AB}}$ follows the mechanism as given below:

$\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{X}]\left[\mathrm{Y}_{2}\right]$
from fast step (i)
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{X}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{X}_{2}\right]} \\& {[X]^{2}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}} \cdot\left[\mathrm{X}_{2}\right]} \\& {[X]=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}}\left[\mathrm{X}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}}\end{aligned}$
From equation (1) \& (2)
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k} \cdot \sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}}\left[\mathrm{X}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{Y}_{2}\right] \\& \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k}^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{X}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{Y}_{2}\right] .\end{aligned}$
Overall order of reaction $=1+0.5=1.5$

question: For the reaction ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{(g) + O(g)}} \to {\rm{2}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}$ if the rate law expression is, rate $=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right][\mathrm{O}]$, the molecularity and order of the reaction respectively are
explanation:
Given reaction is
$\mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
Also, rate law expression is given as:
${\rm{ rate = k}}\left[ {{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right]\left[ {\rm{O}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(1)}}$
Now, since molecularity is simply the sum of reactant molecules in a single step of a chemical reaction. So, molecularity is two for this reaction. On the other hand, order is the sum of the powers raised to the concentration of reactant molecules. From Eq. (1), order is $1+1$ $=2$
$\therefore$ The molecularity is 2 and order is also 2 .

question: For the reaction, $2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ initial concentration of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ is $2.0 \mathrm{molL}^{-1}$ and after 300 min , it is reduced to $1.4 \mathrm{molL}^{-1}$. The rate of production of  $\mathrm{NO}_{2}\left(\mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}\right)$ is
explanation:
$\dfrac{-\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{-[1.4-2]}{300}=\dfrac{0.6}{300}$
$=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
$-\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\dfrac{1}{4} \dfrac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$+\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \times-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}$
$=4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$

question: For the reaction, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{I}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}_{(\mathrm{g})}}}$, the rate of disappearance of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{I}_{2}}}$ is ${\mathrm{2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}}$. If the rate of appearance of HI is given as ${\mathrm{\mathrm{X} \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}}$, then the value of ' X ' is ____ .
explanation:
The average rate of reaction
${\mathrm{=-\dfrac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\dfrac{\Delta[\mathrm{HI}]}{2 \Delta \mathrm{t}}}}$
${\mathrm{2 \times 10^{-4}=\dfrac{\Delta[\mathrm{HI}]}{2 \Delta \mathrm{t}}}}$
${\mathrm{\dfrac{\Delta[\mathrm{HI}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad}}$ Rate of appearance of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{HI}=4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}}$

question:
Assertion: $50 \%$ of a zero order reaction is completed in ${\text{100 sec}}$. therefore, $75 \%$ of the reaction will be completed in ${\text{150 sec}}$.

Reason: The rate constant of a zero order reaction depends upon time.
explanation:
Rate constant of any reaction is constant at constant temperature and does not change with time or concentration.
$\mathrm{t}_{50 \%}=\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{k}}\left\{\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right]-[\mathrm{A}]\right\}$
For zero order raection,
At $\mathrm{t}_{50 \%}[\mathrm{~A}]=\dfrac{\mathrm{A}_{0}}{2}$
$\mathrm{t}_{50 \%}=\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{k}}\left\{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}-\dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{2}\right\}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{2}=100 \mathrm{sec}$
For Completion
$75 \%, \mathrm{t}_{75 \%}=\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{k}}\left\{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}-\dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{2}\right\}=\dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{\mathrm{k}} \times \dfrac{3}{4}$
$=\dfrac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{2 \mathrm{k}} \times \dfrac{3}{2}=100 \times \dfrac{3}{2}=150 \mathrm{sec}$
Thus, option (3) is correct.

question: For the reaction $2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{C}$, rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{A}$ is ${\text{0}}{\text{.076 mol }}{{\text{s}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$. What is the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{B}$ ?
explanation:
${\text{2A + B}} \to {\text{2C}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { Rate }=-\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}} \\& \therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}} \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.076 \mathrm{mols}^{-1}=0.038 \mathrm{mols}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

question:
Ammonia and oxygen react at high temperature as $4 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}+5 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_{(\mathrm{g})}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}$.
If rate of formation of $\mathrm{NO}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ is $3.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, then rate of disappearance of ammonia is
explanation:
Rate of reaction $=-\dfrac{1}{4} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$=-\dfrac{1}{5} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{1}{4} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{NO}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{1}{6} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$\therefore$ Rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}=$ Rate of formation of $\mathrm{NO}$
$ = 3.6 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{mol }}{{\text{L}}^{ - 1}} {{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Average and Instantaneous Rate of Reaction

question: For the reaction $\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}$, what is the relation between $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$ and $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}$
Rate of reaction $=-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=3 \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$

subtopic: Average and Instantaneous Rate of Reaction

question: What is the rate of disappearance of  ${\text{B}}$  in following reaction? $2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{C}$, if rate of appearance of $\mathrm{C}$ is $1.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.
explanation:
${\text{2A + B}} \to {\text{3C}}$
Rate $=-\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{1}{3} \times 1.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
$=4.33 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$

question: What is the rate of appearance of ${\text{Z}}$  in following reaction? $3 \mathrm{x} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}$, if rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{x}$ is ${\text{0}}{\text{.072 mol}}{{\text{s}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$.
explanation:
${\text{3x}} \to {\text{2y + z}}$
Rate of formation $ = - \frac{1}{3}\frac{{{\text{d}}[{\text{x}}]}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{\text{d}}[{\text{y}}]}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = \frac{{{\text{d}}[{\text{z}}]}}{{{\text{dt}}}}$
$\frac{{{\text{d}}[{\text{z}}]}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = \frac{1}{3}(0.072) = 0.024\,\,{\text{mol }}{{\text{s}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$

question:
For the reaction $2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NOCl}$
What is the relation between $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{NO}]}{\mathrm{dt}}$ and
$\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{NOCl}]}{\mathrm{dt}} ?$
explanation:
