question:
For the reaction $4 \mathrm{P}+\mathrm{Q} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{~S}$. Which of the following statement(s) is/are true ?
I. Rate of appearrance of $\mathrm{S}$ is double the rate of the disappearance of $\mathrm{Q}$.
II. Rate of disappearance $\mathrm{Q}$ is one fourth the rate of disappearance of $P$
III. Rate of formation of $\mathrm{R}$ is equal to the rate of formation of $\mathrm{S}$.
Choose the correct option.
explanation:
Given, $4 \mathrm{P}+\mathrm{Q} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{~S}$
Rate $=\dfrac{1}{4}\left[-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{P}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]=\left[-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Q}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]=$
$\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{S}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]$
I. $\left[-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Q}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{S}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right] \Rightarrow$
$\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{S}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]=2\left[-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Q}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]$
II. $\dfrac{1}{4}\left[-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{P}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]=\left[-\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{Q}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]$
III. $\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{S}]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]$
All the given statements are correct

subtopic: Average and Instantaneous Rate of Reaction

question:
Which of the following expressions is correct for the rate of reaction given below?
$5 \mathrm{Br}^{-}{ }_{(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{BrO}_{3(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+6 \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Br}_{2(\mathrm{aq})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})}$
explanation:
For the given reaction,
Rate of reaction $=$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{5}\left[-\dfrac{\Delta\left[B r^{-}\right]}{\Delta t}\right]=\left[-\dfrac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{BrO}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\Delta t}\right] \\& =\dfrac{1}{6}\left[-\dfrac{\Delta\left[H^{+}\right]}{\Delta t}\right]=\dfrac{1}{3}\left[\dfrac{\Delta\left[B r_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}\right]=\dfrac{1}{3}\left[\dfrac{\Delta\left[H_{2} O\right]}{\Delta t}\right]\end{aligned}$
Upon equating the $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$and $\mathrm{H}^{+}$, we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{5}\left[-\dfrac{\Delta\left[B r^{-}\right]}{\Delta t}\right]=\dfrac{1}{6}\left[-\dfrac{\Delta\left[H^{+}\right]}{\Delta t}\right] \\& {\left[-\dfrac{\Delta\left[B r^{-}\right]}{\Delta t}\right]=\dfrac{5}{6}\left[-\dfrac{\Delta\left[H^{+}\right]}{\Delta t}\right]}\end{aligned}$

subtopic: Average and Instantaneous Rate of Reaction

question:
The instantaneous rate of disappearance of the $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$ions in the following reaction is $4.56 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Ms}^{-1}$. Then the rate of appearance of ${{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}$ is:
${\rm{2MnO}}_4^ - + {\rm{10}}{{\rm{I}}^ - } + {\rm{16}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}} \to $
${\rm{2M}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}{\rm{5}}{{\rm{I}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 8}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
explanation:
$-\dfrac{d\left[\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}\right]}{d t}=1.14 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{Ms}^{-1}$
$-\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{d\left[M n O_{4}^{-}\right]}{d t}=+\dfrac{5}{2} \dfrac{d\left[I_{2}\right]}{d t}$
$\dfrac{d\left[I_{2}\right]}{d t}=\dfrac{5}{2} \times 4.56 \times 10^{-3}$
$\dfrac{d\left[I_{2}\right]}{d t}=\dfrac{5}{2}\left[-\dfrac{d\left[\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}\right]}{d t}\right]$
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{d\left[I_{2}\right]}{d t} & =\dfrac{5}{2} \times 4.56 \times 10^{-3} \\& =1.14 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{Ms}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

question: For the reaction, $3 \mathrm{~A}+2 \mathrm{~B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$, the differential rate law can be written as
explanation:
Rate $=-\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{D}]}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { Rate }=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{n}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{m}} \\& \begin{aligned}-\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}} & =-\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{D}]}{\mathrm{dt}} \\& =\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{n}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{m}}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$\text { So, }-\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{n}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{m}}$

question:
Average rate of reaction for the following reaction.
$2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ is written as
explanation:
Average rate of reaction is defined as the speed with which the reactants are converted into products.
For reaction, $2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
$-\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\dfrac{-\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}$
So, average rate of reaction is written as
$\dfrac{-\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}$

subtopic: Average and Instantaneous Rate of Reaction

question:
For the elementary reaction,
$3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ identify the correct relation among the following relations
explanation:
For elementary reaction,
$3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
Rate of reaction $=\dfrac{-1}{3} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{-\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right](\mathrm{g})}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$
For the given options, (2) is correct.
$-\dfrac{2}{3} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$

question:
For the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$,

$\begin{aligned}& \therefore-\dfrac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{d t}=\dfrac{3}{2} \times \dfrac{d\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{d t} \\& =\dfrac{3}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}=3 \times 10^{-4}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned}& -\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}(\mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\dfrac{1}{4} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}(\mathrm{C})}{\mathrm{dt}} \\& -\dfrac{\mathrm{d}(\mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{dt}}=\dfrac{3}{4} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}(\mathrm{C})}{\mathrm{dt}} \\& =\dfrac{3}{4}\left(2.8 \times 10^{-3}\right) \mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

question: For the elementary reaction ${\rm{2S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(}}\,{\rm{g) + }}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(}}\,{\rm{g)}} \to {\rm{2S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{(}}\,{\rm{g)}}$, identify the correct among the following relations
explanation:
For an elementary reaction,
${\text{2S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g) + }}{{\text{o}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{(g)}} \to {\text{2S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{(g),}}$
the instantaneous rate of reaction is expressed as,
${{\text{r}}_{{\text{Inst}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{ - 1}}}}{{\text{2}}}{\text{d}}\frac{{\left[ {{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}}{{{\text{dt}}}}{\text{ = - }}\frac{{{\text{d}}\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}}{{{\text{dt}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}\frac{{{\text{d}}\left[ {{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]}}{{{\text{dt}}}}$
${\text{Thus,}}\,\,\frac{{{\text{d}}\left[ {{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]}}{{{\text{dt}}}}{\text{ = - }}\frac{{{\text{2d}}\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}}{{{\text{dt}}}}$
Hence, option ‘3’ correct.

subtopic: Average and Instantaneous Rate of Reaction

question: For a reaction ${\mathrm{A \xrightarrow{k_{1}} B \xrightarrow{k_{2}} C}}$, if the rate of formation of ${\mathrm{B}}$ is set to be zero then the concentration of ${\mathrm{B}}$ is given by
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{k_{1}} \mathrm{~B} \xrightarrow{k_{2}} C}}$
Net rate of formation of ${\mathrm{B}}$
${\mathrm{=\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}_{1}[\mathrm{~A}]-\mathrm{k}_{2}[B]=0}}$
Given condition, ${\mathrm{\dfrac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=0}}$
So, ${\mathrm{[B]=\dfrac{\mathrm{k}_{1}[\mathrm{~A}]}{\mathrm{k}_{2}}}}$
So, the correct option is (2).

question:
For the set of reactions
${\text{(i) A + B}}\mathop \rightleftharpoons \limits_{{k_2}}^{{k_1}} C,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{(ii) C}} + {\text{B}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{k}}_{\text{3}}}}}{\text{D}}$
${{\text{k}}_{\text{1}}}{\text{[A][B]}} - {{\text{k}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{[C]}} - {{\text{k}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{[C][B]}}$ is equal to
explanation:

the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is equal to
explanation:
$2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \underset{k_{2}}{\stackrel{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$
Rate of reaction $ = - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{\text{d}}\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]}}{{{\text{dt}}}} = {{\text{k}}_1}{\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]^2} - {{\text{k}}_2}\left[ {{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}} \right]$
$\therefore$ Rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$
